blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
غُلِبَتِ ٱلرُّومُ (٢)
The Byzantines has been vanquished (2)
فِىٓ أَدْنَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ (٣)
in a nearby land, but they, after their defeat, will be victorious (3)
فِى بِضْعِ سِنِينَ ۗ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ مِن قَبْلُ وَمِنۢ بَعْدُ ۚ وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (٤)
in a few years. All command belongs to Allah, before this and hereafter, and on that day the faithful will rejoice (4)
بِنَصْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ يَنصُرُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥)
at Allah’s help. He helps whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (5)
وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦)
[This is] a promise of Allah: Allah does not break His promise, but most people do not know. (6)
يَعْلَمُونَ ظَـٰهِرًا مِّنَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٧)
They know just an outward aspect of the life of the world, but they are oblivious of the Hereafter. (7)
أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِم ۗ مَّا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِلِقَآئِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨)
Have they not reflected in their own souls? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term. Indeed many people disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. (8)
أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَثَارُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَعَمَرُوهَآ أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوهَا وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٩)
Have they not travelled in the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more powerful than them, and they plowed the earth and developed it more than they have developed it. Their apostles brought them manifest proofs. So it was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (9)
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓأَىٰٓ أَن كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٠)
Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them. (10)
ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (١١)
Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back, then you will be brought back to Him. (11)
وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يُبْلِسُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (١٢)
And when the Hour sets in, the guilty will despair. (12)
وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُم مِّن شُرَكَآئِهِمْ شُفَعَـٰٓؤُا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٣)
None of those whom they ascribed as partners [to Allah] will intercede for them, and they will disavow their partners. (13)
وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ (١٤)
The day the Hour sets in, they will be divided on that day [in separate groups]: (14)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَهُمْ فِى رَوْضَةٍ يُحْبَرُونَ (١٥)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, they shall be in a garden, rejoicing. (15)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَلِقَآئِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ (١٦)
But as for those who were faithless and denied Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, they will be brought to the punishment. (16)
فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ حِينَ تُمْسُونَ وَحِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ (١٧)
So glorify Allah when you enter evening and when you rise at dawn. (17)
وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ (١٨)
To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth, at nightfall and when you enter noontime. (18)
يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَيُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١٩)
He brings forth the living from the dead, and brings forth the dead from the living, and revives the earth after its death. Likewise, you [too] shall be raised [from the dead]. (19)

(Commentary)

The Surah opens with a promise from Allah, which is that the Romans will overcome the Persians in a few years after their defeat by the Persians at the time of the Surah's revelation. It then transitions from this to the mention of a greater appointment, which is the promise of a Day wherein everyone shall return to Allah, establishing the proof for the Resurrection (المعاد). Subsequently, it turns to mentioning the signs of Lordship (الربوبية) and describes His attributes, the Exalted, which are specific to Him. The Surah then concludes with a promise of victory for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and emphasizes the statement regarding it, as He says: فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَلا يَسْتَخِفَّنَّكَ الَّذِينَ لا يُوقِنُونَ "So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. And do not let yourself be fazed by those who have no conviction." (30:60). Shortly before that, it was said: وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "and it was a must for Us to help the believers." (30:47).

Thus, the purpose of the Surah is the definitive promise from Him, the Exalted, to aid His religion. He preceded this with the victory of the Romans over the Persians within a few years from the time of revelation, so that the fulfillment of this promise might serve as evidence for the fulfillment of that [greater] promise. Likewise, it is used as an argument through the way of reason (طريق العقل) that He will surely fulfill His promise regarding the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ "The Romans have been defeated in a nearby territory" (30:2-3). The Romans (الروم) were a race of people on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea in the West; they possessed a vast empire extending to the lands of Sham (الشامات). A fierce war broke out between them and the Persians in some regions of Sham near the Hijaz, where the Persians were victorious and the Romans were defeated. It appears that "the land" (الأرض) refers to the land of the Hijaz, and the definite article (اللام) is for specific reference (للعهد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ "but after their defeat they will be victorious in a few years." (30:3-4). The first plural pronoun refers to the Romans, as does the third. As for the second, it has been said that it refers to the Persians, meaning: "And the Romans, after the victory of the Persians, shall be victorious." It is also possible that "defeat" (الغلب) is a verbal noun (المصدر) used in the passive sense, with the pronoun referring to the Romans like the pronouns before and after it, so the pronouns do not differ. The meaning would then be: "And the Romans, after their being defeated, shall be victorious." A "few" (البضع) refers to a number from three to nine.

His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَمِنْ بَعْدُ "All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this." (30:4). The words "before" (قبل) and "after" (بعد) are indeclinable with a damma (مبنيان على الضم) because there is an omitted genitive construct (مضاف إليه مقدر). The estimation is: "To Allah belongs the command from before the Romans were defeated and from after they are victorious." He commands whatever He wills, so He aids whom He wills and forsakes whom He wills.

It has also been said: The meaning is that to Allah belongs the command before their being victors—which is the time of their being defeated—and after their being defeated—which is the time of their being victors. That is, the time of their defeat and the time of their victory. However, the first meaning is more probable, if not the definitively preferred one.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللهِ يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "And on that day the believers will rejoice in Allah’s help. He helps whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (30:4-5). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) is connected to "will rejoice" (يفرح), as is His saying "He helps" (يَنْصُرُ). The meaning is: "And on the day when the Romans are victorious, the believers will rejoice in Allah's aiding of the Romans." Then He began a new sentence and said: "He helps whomever He wishes" (يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ) as an affirmation of His saying: "All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this."

His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful"—meaning, He is the Mighty (عزيز) who strengthens with His help whom He wills, and the Merciful (رحيم) who singles out for His mercy whom He wills.

Regarding the verse, there are other weak views mentioned by commentators: Among them is that His saying "And on that day" (وَيَوْمَئِذٍ) is coordinated (عطف) with His saying "before" (مِنْ قَبْلُ), and the intent is the encompassment of His sovereignty, the Exalted, over all three times: the past, the future, and the present. It is as if it were said: "To Allah belongs the command before, and after, and on that day," then a new sentence begins: "the believers will rejoice in Allah's help." The flaw in this is that it destroys the harmony (انسجام) of the verse and disconnects its end from its beginning.

Another view is that His saying "in the help" (بِنَصْرِ) is connected to His saying "the believers" (الْمُؤْمِنُونَ) rather than "will rejoice" (يَفْرَحُ), and it indicates by situational implication (الملازمة المقامية) that the victory of the Romans is by help from Allah.

The flaw in this is that its implication would be that the believers rejoice both on the day of the Persian victory and the day of the Roman victory, for in every victory there is help, and all help is from Allah. The Exalted said: وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "and help comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (3:126). Thus, restricting the believers' joy in the help to the day of the Roman victory would be a preference without a differentiator (ترجيح بلا مرجح); so understand this.

Another view is that the "help of Allah" refers to the victory of the believers over the polytheists on the day of Badr, rather than the victory of the Romans over the Persians, even if the two victories coincided in time. It is as if it were said: "The Romans will be victorious in a few years, and on the day they are victorious, the believers will defeat the polytheists, so they will rejoice in Allah's help to them."

The flaw in this is that this meaning does not harmonize with His subsequent saying: "He helps whomever He wishes" (يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

Another view is that the "help" refers to the aiding of the believers through the truthfulness of their report regarding the Roman victory. It has also been said: The help is the dominance of some disbelievers over others, the fracturing of their unity, and the breaking of their might. These and similar views are positions not to be reckoned with.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعْدَ اللهِ لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "[It is] Allah’s promise: Allah does not break His promise, but most people do not know." (30:6). "Allah's promise" (وَعْدَ اللهِ) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) whose governing verb is omitted; the estimation is: "Allah promised a promise." Breaking a promise (إخلاف الوعد) is the opposite of fulfilling it. His saying "Allah's promise" is an emphasis and affirmation of the previous promise in His saying "they will be victorious" and "the believers will rejoice," just as His saying "Allah does not break His promise" is an emphasis and affirmation of His saying "Allah's promise."

His saying: لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ "Allah does not break His promise" is like His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ "Indeed Allah does not break His promise" (13:31). Breaking a promise, even if not inherently (بالذات) repugnant—as it might be good in cases of necessity—yet He, the Glorified, is not compelled by any necessity, so breaking a promise is never good from Him in any state.

Furthermore, breaking a promise always entails a deficiency (النقص), and deficiency is impossible for Him, the Exalted.

Moreover, He, the Exalted, has informed in His speech that He does not break His promise, and He is the most truthful of the truthful, and He is the one who says—exalted is the Speaker: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ "and I speak the truth" (38:84).

His saying: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "but most people do not know"—meaning they are ignorant of His affairs, the Exalted; they do not trust His promise and they measure Him against their own kind, who speak truth and lie, and who fulfill and break promises.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَهُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ "They know [only] an outward appearance of the life of this world, and they are oblivious of the Hereafter." (30:7). The sentence "They know" (يَعْلَمُونَ), according to what is mentioned in al-Kashshaf, is a substitute (بدل) for His saying "they do not know" (لا يَعْلَمُونَ). In this substitution, there is a subtle point: he substituted it and made it stand in its place to teach you that there is no difference between the absence of knowledge, which is ignorance, and the presence of knowledge that does not transcend this world. End quote.

It has also been said: The sentence is an exception (استثنائية) to explain the cause of their ignorance that Allah's promise is true, that to Allah belongs the command before and after, and that He aids the believers over the disbelievers. End quote. This is more apparent.

The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "an outward appearance" (ظاهِراً) is for belittlement (للتحقير). The outward of the life of this world is what stands in contrast to its inward (باطنها); it is that which their external senses perceive of the adornment of life, which guides them to acquire it, devote themselves to it, cling to it, and forget what lies beyond it of the life of the Hereafter and the knowledge related to it, being oblivious to that which contains their good and benefit in the true sense of the word.

It has been said: "Appearance" (الظهور) in the verse means "passing away" (الزوال), citing as evidence the verse: The slanderers reproached her that I love her, And that is a complaint whose shame is passing (ظاهر) from you. The meaning would then be: "They know a passing matter that has no permanence," but this is a rare usage of the meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ما خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "Have they not reflected in their own souls? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term..." (30:8). The intent of creating the heavens and the earth and what is between them—which is the totality of the witnessed world—"with truth" (بالحق) is that they were not created in vain (عبثاً) without a purpose beyond them, such that they exist and perish, then exist and perish, without an aim or goal. Rather, He, the Exalted, only created them for a purpose that follows from them.

Furthermore, the world with its parts is not eternal in existence without an end, such that it might be possible for every subsequent part to be the purpose for the preceding part, and every future thing to be a successor to its past. Rather, it is, with its parts, perishable and vanishing. Thus, there is an intended purpose for the creation of the world that will manifest after the annihilation of the world. This meaning is what is intended by qualifying His saying "Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" with His saying "and for a specified term" (وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى) after qualifying it with His saying "except with truth" (إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ).

So His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ "Have they not reflected in their own souls?"—the interrogation is for expressing wonder (للتعجيب). Their being "in their own souls" is a metaphorical allusion (استعارة كنائية) to peace of mind and presence of thought. It is as if, when they are occupied with worldly affairs, striving for livelihood, and having disturbed minds, they are absent from themselves. Thus, when their minds are present, they are present and settled within themselves, and their reflection then becomes focused and not scattered, guiding them to the truth and directing them to reality.

It has been said: The intent of their reflecting "in their own souls" is that they should reflect on the creation of their own selves; that each one of them is originated (محدث), and that which is originated (المحدث) requires an Originator (محدث) who is Eternal, Living, Powerful, All-Knowing, and All-Wise. He does not create what He creates in vain, but for a desired purpose. This purpose does not return to Him Himself due to His absolute self-sufficiency (الغنى المطلق), but rather to the creation, which is the reward. This reward can only be for righteous deeds, so there must be a legislated religion (دين مشرع) that distinguishes righteous deeds from evil ones. Thus, there must be an abode in which they are tested—which is this world—and an abode in which they are rewarded—which is the Hereafter.

The flaw in this is that the sentence—I mean His saying "Have they not reflected in their own souls"—is inherently capable of being intended for this meaning, but the connection of His saying "Allah did not create the heavens..." etc., to it precludes this, as it would necessitate the breakdown of the connection due to the lack of link between the beginning of the verse and its end on this assumption.

His saying: ما خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with truth and for a specified term" is the thought upon which they must deeply reflect within themselves. Its affirmation, based on what preceded, is that Allah, the Glorified, did not create this world, neither in its entirety nor in its parts, except as a creation accompanied by truth (بالحق)—meaning for a real purpose, not in vain without a goal—and until a specific term. Thus, nothing of it remains indefinitely; rather, it perishes and ceases. If each of its parts and the whole is a creation possessing a purpose that follows from it, and none of it is eternal in existence, then its purpose must follow from it after the cessation of its existence and its annihilation. This is the Hereafter (الآخرة) which will manifest after the passing of this world and its annihilation.

His saying: وَإِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكافِرُونَ "Indeed many of the people are disbelievers in the encounter with their Lord." (30:8) is presented in a manner of expressing wonder, just as the verse began with an interrogative of wonder. The intent of "the encounter with Allah" (لقاء الله) is the return to Him in the Resurrection. It was expressed as "encounter" (اللقاء) to increase the wonder at their disbelief in it; for how is it possible that they begin from Him and then do not end with Him? Therefore, He emphasized it with "Indeed" (إن) to indicate that disbelief in the Resurrection is something that, in itself, should not be believed.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ "Have they not traveled through the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them?" (30:9) to the end of the verse. When He mentioned the disbelief of many people in the Resurrection—which is a matter that renders the true religion futile—He reminded them of the state of the disbelieving nations and the evil punishment they reached, so that perhaps they might take heed and turn back from the disbelief they are upon. "Tilling" (إثارة) the earth is turning it over for cultivation, development, and the like.

His saying: وَلكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ "but they used to wrong themselves" (30:9)—meaning through disbelief and acts of disobedience.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ وَكانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ "Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was the worst [fate], for they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them." (30:10). This is an explanation of what the affair of those wrongdoers ended in, which is why He used "then" (ثم). "Fate" (عاقِبَةَ) is in the accusative case (بالنصب) as the predicate (خبر) of "was" (كان), and its subject (اسمه) is "the worst" (السُّواى); the predicate was placed before it to signify restriction (الحصر). "Committed misdeeds" (أَساؤُا) is detached from an object, meaning they performed evil deeds. "The worst" (السوآى) is the state that grieves its possessor, and what is intended by it is the evil punishment. "For they denied the signs of Allah" (أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ) involves the omission of the "lam" of causality (لام التعليل), and the estimation is: "because of their denial of Allah's signs and their deriding them."

The meaning is: Then the evil punishment was that which the affair of those who committed evil ended in; they had no other fate because of their denial of Allah's signs and their deriding them.

It has been said: "The worst" (السُّواى) is the object (مفعول) of His saying "committed misdeeds" (أَساؤُا), and the predicate of "was" (كان) is His saying "that they denied" (أَنْ كَذَّبُوا) etc. The meaning would be that acts of disobedience led them to disbelief by denying Allah's signs and deriding them.

The flaw in this is: while it is a correct meaning in itself, the first meaning is more suitable for the context, because the context is one of taking heed and warning. What is suitable for it is the explanation of their disobedience ending in the evil punishment, not their scattered acts of disobedience ending in denial and derision, which are the greatest of them.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ "Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back, then you will be brought back to Him." (30:11). After mentioning the proof and the denial of many people, He summarized the statement in its conclusion, which is that the beginning and the return are in His hand, the Glorified, and everyone will return to Him. What is intended by "creation" (الخلق) is the created beings (المخلوقون), which is why He returned the plural pronoun to it in "you will be brought back" (تُرْجَعُونَ).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُبْلِسُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ "On the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will be struck with despair." (30:12). He mentioned the state of the guilty after the setting in of the Hour, which is the hour of return to Him, the Exalted, for reckoning and recompense. "Despair" (الإبلاس) is losing hope in Allah, and in it lies all wretchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ مِنْ شُرَكائِهِمْ شُفَعاءُ وَكانُوا بِشُرَكائِمْ كافِرِينَ "None of those whom they associated [with Allah] will intercede for them, and they will disown their partners." (30:13). He means that despite their despair of mercy from the side of their own deeds, they are also despairing of their deities whom they took as partners to Allah and worshipped so that they might intercede for them with Allah, as they used to say in the world: "These are our intercessors with Allah." And they were disbelievers (كافرين)—concealers—of the worship of their partners.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ "On the day when the Hour sets in, on that day they will be separated." (30:14) until His saying: مُحْضَرُونَ "will be presented [for punishment]" (30:16). It is said in al-Majma': "The garden" (الروضة) is the orchard that is ultimate in appearance and fragrance. End quote. It is said in al-Mufradat: "The mark" (الحبر) is the beautiful effect—until he said—and His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: فِي رَوْضَةٍ يُحْبَرُونَ "they will be made happy in a garden" (30:15) means they will rejoice until the mark of their bliss appears upon them. End quote.

The intent of the separation of the creation on that day is the distinction of the righteous believers from the guilty, and the entry of the latter into the Fire and the former into Paradise, as indicated by the two following verses.

The necessity of this distinction and separation in existence is what Allah, the Glorified, took as a proof for the establishment of the Resurrection where He said: أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ "Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgment that they make!" (45:21).

His saying, the Exalted: فَسُبْحانَ اللهِ حِينَ تُمْسُونَ وَحِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ "So celebrate Allah’s glory when you enter the evening and when you enter the morning. To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth, at nightfall and when you enter the noon." (30:17-18). When He mentioned that He originates the creation then brings them back and returns them for His encounter, then separates them into two groups: the people of Paradise and bliss, and the people of the Fire and punishment—as for the people of Paradise, they are the believers who perform righteous deeds, and as for the people of the Fire, they are the disbelievers who deny Allah's signs—and He mentioned that they were in the world people of power and luxury, but they forgot the Hereafter, denied Allah's signs, and derided them until their affair ended in the evil punishment, the punishment of eradication, as a recompense for their wronging themselves; and Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves.

It follows from this that in the abode of creation there is a divine governance (تدبيراً إلهياً) that is firm, righteous, and beautiful in the most beautiful way, and that for man, over the succession of times and ages, there are sins and errors ranging from evil belief regarding his Lord, taking partners for Him, and denying His encounter, to other acts of disobedience.

He concluded the speech by glorifying Him (تسبيحه) whenever a time is renewed after another, and praising Him (تحميده) for His handiwork and governance in the heavens and the earth, which is the totality of the witnessed world. Thus, He, the Glorified, is transcendent (منزه) above these false beliefs and wicked deeds, and is praised (محمود) in all that He has created and governed in the heavens and the earth.

From this, it becomes clear: First, that the glorification (التسبيح) and praise (التحميد) in the two verses are an initiation of transcendence and commendation from Him, the Exalted, not from anyone else, such that the meaning would be: "Say: Glory be to Allah" and "Say: Praise be to Allah." For His glorification and praise of Himself have been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ "Immune is your Lord, the Lord of Might" (37:180) and His saying: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant" (25:1).

Second, that the intent of glorification and praise is their absolute meaning (معناهما المطلق), rather than the daily obligatory prayers, as is held by most of those who say that the word "Say" is estimated. The meaning is: "Say: Glory be to Allah" and "Say: Praise be to Allah."

Third, that His saying: وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth" is a parenthetical clause (معترضة) situated between the coordinated elements. His saying: وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ "at nightfall and when you enter the noon" are coordinated with the position of "when you enter the evening" (حِينَ تُمْسُونَ), not with His saying "in the heavens and the earth," such that the evening and morning would be specific to glorification, and the heavens, earth, nightfall, and noon specific to praise. Rather, the times and what is in them are for glorification, and the places and what is in them are for praise.

The context indicates that what is in the heavens and the earth of creation and command belongs to Allah, necessitating by its beauty praise and commendation for Allah, the Glorified; and that for man, throughout the ages and the changing of times and moments, there is polytheism and disobedience from which His holy presence, the Exalted and Sanctified, is transcendent.

Yes, there is another consideration here in which praise and glorification overlap, which is that the times and moments, in their changing and passing, are among what is in the heavens and the earth, so by their existence they commend Allah, the Exalted. Then, everything in the heavens and the earth, by its poverty toward Him, the Exalted, its lowliness before Him, and its deficiency relative to His perfection, the Exalted, glorifies Him, as He said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise" (18:44). However, this consideration is not what is intended in the two verses we are currently discussing.

The commentators have other scattered opinions regarding the two verses; we have pointed out the important ones among them in the views we have presented.

The change in the context in His saying: وَعَشِيًّا "at nightfall" is because from "nightfall" (العشي) no verb was formed on the pattern of if'al (إفعال), unlike "evening" (المساء), "morning" (الصباح), and "noon" (الظهيرة), where imsā’, isbāh, and idhhār were formed, meaning entering into the evening, morning, and noon. So it has been said.

The address in the two verses in His saying: "you enter the evening" (تُمْسُونَ), "you enter the morning" (تُصْبِحُونَ), and "you enter the noon" (تُظْهِرُونَ) is not a case of grammatical shift (التفات) at all, but rather a generalization of the address that has been directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) since the Surah began. The meaning is:

If the matter is in this way, then Allah is transcendent when you, O assembly of humans, enter into evening, and when you enter into morning, and at nightfall, and when you enter into noon; and to Him belongs beautiful praise in the heavens and the earth.

Similar to this generalization is what appeared previously in His saying: "then you will be brought back to Him" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ) and subsequently in His saying: "and thus you will be brought forth" (وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ).

His saying, the Exalted: يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ وَيُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ "He brings forth the living from the dead and He brings forth the dead from the living, and He revives the earth after its death. Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth." (30:19). The apparent meaning of bringing forth the living from the dead and vice versa is the creation of living beings from the dead earth, then the turning of living beings into dead earth. It has also been interpreted as the creation of the believer from the disbeliever and the creation of the disbeliever from the believer, for the believer is considered living and the disbeliever dead. The Exalted said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light..." (6:122).

As for the revival of the earth after its death, it is the earth's recovery and its rejoicing with vegetation in the spring and summer after its dormancy in the autumn and winter. His saying: وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ "Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth"—meaning you shall be resurrected and brought forth from your graves with a new life, like the revival of the earth after its death. The interpretation of the like of the beginning and end of this verse has preceded repeatedly.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it hasan), al-Nasa'i, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in al-Kabir, al-Hakim (who graded it sahih), Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il, and al-Diya' narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: الم غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ (Alif, Lam, Mim. The Romans have been defeated). He said: "They were defeated and then they were victorious."

He said: "The polytheists loved that the Persians should prevail over the Romans because they were people of idols, while the Muslims loved that the Romans should prevail over the Persians because they were people of the Book. They mentioned this to Abu Bakr, and Abu Bakr mentioned it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'Indeed, they shall be victorious.' Abu Bakr mentioned it to them, and they said: 'Set a term between us and you; if we prevail, we shall have such and such, and if you prevail, you shall have such and such.' So he set for them five years, but they did not prevail. Abu Bakr mentioned that to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: 'Why did you not set it'—I think he said—'under ten?' The Romans were victorious after that. That is His saying: الم غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ (Alif, Lam, Mim. The Romans have been defeated); they were defeated and then they were victorious afterward."

Allah says: لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَمِنْ بَعْدُ ـ وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللهِ (All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this. And on that day the believers will rejoice in Allah’s help). Sufyan said: "I heard that they were victorious on the day of Badr."

I say: Regarding this meaning, there are other narrations with somewhat different contents. In some of them, the gambling (المقامرة) was between Abu Bakr and Ubayy ibn Khalaf; in some, it was between the Muslims and the polytheists, with Abu Bakr representing the Muslims and Ubayy representing the polytheists; in some, it was between the two groups; and in some, between Abu Bakr and the polytheists, as in this narration.

Furthermore, the term set was three years in some, five in some, six in some, and seven years in others.

In some, the term set initially expired in Mecca, which was seven years, so Abu Bakr extended it for them by two years by order of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and then the Romans were victorious. In others, it is contrary to this.

Then, in some, the second term expired in Mecca, and in others, it expired after the Hijrah, and the Roman victory was on the day of Badr; in others, on the day of al-Hudaybiyyah.

In some, when Abu Bakr won the bet regarding the Roman victory, he took the stakes (الخطر) from them, which was a hundred young camels, and brought them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "It is ill-gotten (سحت); give it in charity."

What the narrations agree upon is that he gambled with them and won, and the gambling was at the suggestion of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This was justified by the claim that it was before the prohibition of gambling, as it was prohibited along with wine in Surah al-Ma'idah, which was revealed at the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era.

It has been established by what we presented in the commentary on the verse of wine and gambling (الميسر) that wine was prohibited from the beginning of the Mission, and it was known from the religion that wine and adultery are prohibited.

Moreover, wine and gambling are among "sin" (الإثم) by the text of the verse in al-Baqarah: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ "They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them...’" (2:219). And sin is prohibited by the text of the verse in al-A'raf: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ "Say, ‘My Lord has only prohibited indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and sin and undue aggression...’" (7:33). Surah al-A'raf is among the early Surahs revealed in Mecca. Thus, it is impossible that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would suggest gambling.

Even assuming the prohibition was delayed until the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era, his saying (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Abu Bakr when he brought the stakes to him—that it is ill-gotten (سحت), then his saying: "Give it in charity"—poses a problem. There is no way to rectify any of that by jurisprudential standards (الموازين الفقهية), and they have labored to justify it in ways that only increase the difficulty.

Furthermore, what is in the narration—that the Persians were idol worshippers—does not agree with the state of those people, for although they were polytheists, they did not take idols.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ـ وَهُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ (They know [only] an outward appearance of the life of this world, and they are oblivious of the Hereafter). He said: "They see the present of the world and are heedless of the Hereafter."

In al-Khisal, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ (Have they not traveled through the land). He said: "Have they not looked into the Qur'an?"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ (On the day when the Hour sets in, on that day they will be separated). He said: "To Paradise and the Fire."

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنْ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ إِذَآ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ تَنتَشِرُونَ (٢٠)
Of His signs is that He created you from dust, then, behold, you are humans scattering [all over]! (20)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا لِّتَسْكُنُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُم مَّوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١)
And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them, and He ordained affection and mercy between you. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (21)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوَٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْعَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٢)
Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the difference of your languages and colours. There are indeed signs in that for those who know. (22)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ مَنَامُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱبْتِغَآؤُكُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٢٣)
And of His signs is your sleep by night and day, and your pursuit of His bounty. There are indeed signs in that for a people who listen. (23)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ يُرِيكُمُ ٱلْبَرْقَ خَوْفًا وَطَمَعًا وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَيُحْىِۦ بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٤)
And of His signs is that He shows you the lightning, arousing fear and hope, and He sends down water from the sky, and with it revives the earth after its death. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (24)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَن تَقُومَ ٱلسَّمَآءُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۚ ثُمَّ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ دَعْوَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ إِذَآ أَنتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ (٢٥)
And of His signs is that the sky and the earth stand by His command, and then, when He calls you forth from the earth, behold, you will come forth. (25)
وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ (٢٦)
To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him. (26)

(Commentary)

In this section, several verses are mentioned that demonstrate His Exaltedness’s oneness in Lordship (الربوبية) — the exclusive authority of Allah to sustain, regulate, and govern all creation — and Divinity (الألوهية) — the unique right of Allah alone to be worshipped. Reference is made therein to the interweaving and overlapping of creation (الخلق) and regulation (التدبير), so that it becomes clear that Lordship, in the sense of possessing the authority of regulation, and Divinity, in the sense of being the Truthful Object of Worship, are not deserved by anyone except Allah, Who created all things and brought them into existence. This is contrary to the claim of the idolater (الوثني) that creation belongs to Allah alone, while regulation and worship belong to the lords of the idols so that they may be intercessors for them with Allah, and that He, the Glorified, is merely the Lord of lords and the God of gods.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ» "Of His signs is that He created you from dust, then, behold, you are humans scattering [everywhere]." (30:20). What is meant by creation from dust is the ultimate origin of human creation from the earth; for the stages of human formation—whether from a fetus-lump (مضغة), a clinging mass (علقة), a drop of fluid (نطفة), or otherwise—are terrestrial compounds that trace back to earthly elements.

And His saying: «ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ» "then, behold, you are humans scattering [everywhere]"; the word "behold" (إِذا) indicates suddenness (فجائية), meaning it suddenly happens that you become human beings spreading across the earth. That is, He creates you from earthly compositions from which another similar dead earthly existence would be expected, yet suddenly and all at once, it becomes a human being possessed of life and intellectual perception (شعور عقلي), spreading through the earth to manage the affairs of life. Thus, His saying: «ثُمَّ إِذا أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ تَنْتَشِرُونَ» carries the same meaning as His saying: «ثُمَّ أَنْشَأْناهُ خَلْقاً آخَرَ» "then We produced him as another creation" (23:14).

Thus, the creation of man—meaning the gathering of his parts from the earth and their composition—is a sign (آية); and the transformation of this aggregate into a human being with life and intellectual perception is another sign, or multiple signs, pointing to a Living, All-Knowing Maker Who regulates the affair and conducts this wondrous system.

It has become clear through this meaning that "then" (ثُمَّ) denotes a rank-based sequence (التراخي الرتبي), and the clause is coordinated with His saying: «خَلَقَكُمْ» (He created you), not with the phrase «أَنْ خَلَقَكُمْ» (that He created you).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها» "And of His signs is that He created for you mates from your own selves that you may take comfort in them" (30:21) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "The term mate (زوج) is applied to each one of a pair, male and female, among animals that mate; and it is applied to each of any two companions, whether among animals or otherwise. The Exalted said: «فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَالْأُنْثى» 'and made of him the two sexes, the male and the female' (75:39), and He said: «وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ» 'and your mate, the Garden' (2:35). The word zawjah (زوجة) is a poor linguistic usage, and its plural is zawjat (زوجات)..." until he said: "and the plural of zawj is azwaj (أزواج)." End quote.

So His saying: «أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً لِتَسْكُنُوا إِلَيْها» means He created for your sake—or for your benefit—companions from your own kind. This is because each of the man and the woman is equipped with a reproductive system whose function is completed only by the pairing with the other; through their union, the matter of procreation and lineage is fulfilled. Thus, each of them is deficient in themselves, in need of the other, and from the union, a complete single entity is formed capable of procreating. Because of this deficiency and need, each moves toward the other until, when joined, they find rest (سكن) in them; for every deficient thing yearns for its perfection, and every needy thing inclines toward that which removes its poverty. This is the desire (الشبق) implanted in each of these two companions.

And His saying: «وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَرَحْمَةً» "and He ordained affection and mercy between you." Affection (المودة) is like love (الحب) whose effect is manifest in the realm of action. The relationship of affection to love is like the relationship of outward humility (الخضوع), whose effect is manifest in action, to inward submissiveness (الخشوع), which is a type of psychological state resulting from [perceiving] greatness and majesty.

And mercy (الرحمة) is a type of psychological state resulting from witnessing the deprivation of a deprived person from perfection and their need to have their deficiency removed; it calls the merciful one to save them from deprivation and rectify their lack.

Among the most prominent instances of affection and mercy is the domestic society (المجتمع المنزلي); for the two spouses are bound together by affection and love, and together—especially the wife—they show mercy to the young children because they see their weakness and inability to perform the necessary tasks to fulfill vital needs. Thus, they undertake the duty of protecting, guarding, feeding, clothing, sheltering, and raising them. Were it not for this mercy, lineage would be severed and the species would never survive.

A parallel to this affection and mercy is observed in the larger civil society (المجتمع المدني) among its members; for one individual finds sociability with another through affection and shows mercy to the poor, the disabled, and the weak who cannot fulfill the requirements of life.

The intended meaning of affection and mercy in the verse is either the former [domestic] sense, as suggested by the context, or the latter [civil] sense, as suggested by the generality of the verse.

And His saying: «لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ» "There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect." This is because if they reflect upon the ontological foundations (الأصول التكوينية) that drive man to form society—namely maleness and femaleness which call for domestic association, and affection and mercy which drive civil association—and then consider what results from this association in terms of the survival of the species and the perfection of man in his worldly and otherworldly life, they would discover such wonders of divine signs in the regulation of this species' affairs that would baffle their intellects and amaze their minds.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَاخْتِلافُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوانِكُمْ» "Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the difference of your languages and colours" (30:22) to the end of the verse. It appears that the intended meaning of the difference of languages (الألسن) is the difference of tongues such as Arabic, Persian, Urdu, and others; and by the difference of colors (الألوان) is meant the difference of nations in their complexions, such as whiteness, blackness, yellowness, and redness.

It is also possible to derive the difference of languages from the perspective of tones, voices, and modes of speech and articulation; and the difference of colors as the difference of every two individuals among human beings in terms of color if scrutinized closely, as maintained by the scientists of this field.

Thus, those who investigate the macrocosm (العالم الكبير) discover in the system of creation subtle signs indicating that the making and bringing into existence, along with the system governing it, cannot be established except by Allah and does not terminate except in Him.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ مَنامُكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَابْتِغاؤُكُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ» "And of His signs is your sleep by night and day, and your pursuit of His bounty" (30:23) to the end of the verse. Bounty (الفضل) is that which exceeds the amount of need; it is also applied to a gift because the giver only gives what is surplus to his own need. In the noble verse, it refers to sustenance (الرزق); thus, the pursuit of bounty is the seeking of sustenance.

In the creation of man with active powers that drive him to seek sustenance and fulfill the needs of life for survival through movement and effort, then guiding him to rest and stillness to remove the fatigue of effort and renew the equipment of his powers, and the designation of the alternating night and day for effort and rest, and the causation of the existence of night and day through celestial positions involving the earth and the sun—in all this are beneficial signs for one who possesses an attentive ear (سمع واع) that understands what it hears; for when he finds it to be the truth, he follows it.

The author of al-Kashshaf said regarding the verse: "This is of the category of involution (اللف) and its arrangement is: 'Of His signs is your sleep by night and your pursuit of His bounty by day,' except that He separated the first two paired elements with the latter two because they are times, and the time and what occurs within it are like a single thing, with the involution aiding the sense of unity. It is also permissible that your sleep is intended in both times and your pursuit in both of them, but the first view is more apparent due to its repetition in the Qur'an, and the soundest meanings are those indicated by the Qur'an." End quote.

From what has preceded, the meaning of concluding the verse with His saying: «إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ» "There are indeed signs in that for a people who listen" has become clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَيُحْيِي بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها» "And of His signs is that He shows you the lightning, [evoking] fear and hope, and He sends down water from the sky, and with it He revives the earth after its death" (30:24). It appears that the verb [يريكم] stands in the place of a verbal noun (المصدر); therefore, it was not preceded by the particle an (أن المصدرية) as were His sayings: «أَنْ خَلَقَكُمْ» and «أَنْ خَلَقَ لَكُمْ». Treating a verb as a verbal noun is excellent Arabic usage, and upon this is based the well-known proverb: "Hearing of the Mu'aydi is better than seeing him." There is no harm in interpreting His speech, the Exalted, upon this, for He employs various modes of expression at the opening of these verses, such as His sayings: «مَنامُكُمْ» (your sleep), «يُرِيكُمُ» (He shows you), and «أَنْ تَقُومَ» (that [it] stands).

It has been suggested regarding His saying: «يُرِيكُمُ» that it involves the omission of the particle an, the implied meaning being "that He shows you the lightning," which is supported by the subjunctive reading (النصب) of yuriyakum.

It has also been suggested that it involves the omission of a prefixed noun (المضاف), the implied meaning being: "And of His signs is the sign of showing you the lightning." Another suggestion is that the implied meaning is: "And of His signs is the sign of lightning," then a new sentence begins: "He shows you the lightning..." It has also been suggested that «مِنْ آياتِهِ» is connected to His saying: «يُرِيكُمُ», the implied meaning being: "And He shows you the lightning from His signs." Finally, it has been suggested that «مِنْ آياتِهِ» is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the lightning, the implied meaning being: "And He shows you the lightning while the lightning is among His signs."

These are various views, and their far-fetched nature is not hidden from you, especially since some of them remove the speech in the verse from conformity with the context of the preceding similar verses, such as the last two views.

And His saying: «خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً» means fear of the thunderbolt and hope for rain. And His saying: «وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَيُحْيِي بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها» has had its explanation repeated frequently. And His saying: «إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ» "There are indeed signs in that for a people who exercise their reason" means that the people of intellect understand that there is a Divine care (عناية) connected to these benefits, and it is not merely a matter of coincidence or chance (صدفة).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ تَقُومَ السَّماءُ وَالْأَرْضُ بِأَمْرِهِ ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ دَعْوَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ إِذا أَنْتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ» "And of His signs is that the sky and the earth stand by His command. Then, when He calls you with a call from the earth, behold, you will come forth" (30:25). Standing (القيام) is the opposite of sitting. Since it is the most balanced of human states, through which one is capable of most actions, it is used metaphorically for the stability of a thing and its settlement upon its most balanced state, just as it is used metaphorically for the regulation of an affair (تدبير الأمر). The Exalted said: «أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ» "Is He who is the overseer of every soul as to what it earns [like those who are not]?" (14:33).

What is meant by the standing of the heaven and the earth by a command from Allah is their stability in their state of motion, rest, change, and constancy by His command, the Exalted. He has defined His command by His saying: «إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ» "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82).

And His saying: «ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ دَعْوَةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ إِذا أَنْتُمْ تَخْرُجُونَ»; the first "when" (إِذا) is conditional (شرطية), and the second "behold" (إِذا) is for suddenness (فجائية), standing in the place of the particle fa (فاء الجزاء) that introduces the result. The phrase "from the earth" (مِنَ الْأَرْضِ) is connected to His saying "a call" (دَعْوَةً). The sentence is coordinated with the position of the first sentence because the intended meaning of the sentence—namely His saying: «ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ» etc.—is the Resurrection (البعث) and the return to Allah; it is not counted among the signs themselves, but rather the sentence is an announcement of a matter for which evidence was provided previously and will be provided hereafter.

As for the claim of one who says that the sentence is interpreted as a single noun and is coordinated with «أَنْ تَقُومَ», with the implied meaning being: "And of His signs is the standing of the heaven and the earth by His command, then your coming forth when He calls you with a call from the earth"—

This would necessitate that the Resurrection be counted among the signs, whereas it is not. Furthermore, the Resurrection is one of the three fundamental principles (الأصول الثلاثة) for which the signs are used as evidence; it is not itself used as evidence for Monotheism (التوحيد), for example. Rather, if evidence were to be used, it would be Monotheism as evidence for it. So understand that.

Since the mentioned signs—the creation of humans from dust, their creation as mates, the difference of their languages and colors, their sleep and pursuit of His bounty, the showing of lightning, and the sending down of water from the sky—are all signs pertaining to the regulation of human affairs, the intended meaning of His saying: «أَنْ تَقُومَ السَّماءُ وَالْأَرْضُ», aided by the context, is the stability of the heaven and the earth in their natural positions and normal states, being suitable for the life of the human species connected to them. His saying: «ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ» etc., follows that in a sequential order of delay; that is, their coming forth from the earth is delayed after this "standing" and coincides with their destruction, as informed by many verses in various places of His speech, the Exalted.

It also becomes clear from this that the intended meaning of His previous saying: «وَمِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ» is their creation from the perspective of how they are connected to human life and benefit it.

The mentioned verses have been arranged starting from the beginning of human creation and formation, then his classification into two types: male and female, then the connection of his existence to the heaven and the earth and the difference of their languages and colors, then the effort in seeking sustenance and the stillness of sleep, then the showing of lightning and the sending down of rains, until it concludes with the standing of the heaven and the earth until an appointed term, so that the span of life decreed for this human species may be completed, followed by the Resurrection. This is part of the subtle points (النكات) in the arrangement of these signs.

The verse endings (الفواصل)—namely His sayings: "they reflect" (يَتَفَكَّرُونَ), "for the knowledgeable" (لِلْعالِمِينَ), "they listen" (يَسْمَعُونَ), and "they exercise reason" (يَعْقِلُونَ)—have been arranged in this order because man reflects, thereby becoming knowledgeable; then, when he hears something of the truths, he attends to it, and then he reasons it. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلٌّ لَهُ قانِتُونَ» "To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him" (30:26). The mentioned signs were aimed at proving His Lordship and Divinity, as previously indicated. When the discourse reached the mention of the Resurrection and the return to Allah, it was followed by the proof of its possibility, with the argument being drawn from the creation and regulation mentioned in the previous verses.

So His saying: «وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ» is an indication of the encompassment of His true ownership (الملك الحقيقي) over all who are in the heavens and the earth, who are those to be gathered unto Him. This is because their existence from every aspect is sustained by Him, the Exalted, in a state of poverty and need (فقر وحاجة); they have no independence or self-sufficiency from Him in any way. This is the true ownership whose effect is the permissibility of the Owner to dispose of His property as He wills; thus, it is for Him, the Exalted, to dispose of those He owns by transferring them from the worldly realm (النشأة الدنيا) to the hereafter (النشأة الآخرة).

He emphasized this with His saying: «كُلٌّ لَهُ قانِتُونَ» "All are obedient to Him." Obedience (القنوت) is the persistence in obedience (الطاعة) along with submissiveness (الخضوع)—as mentioned by al-Raghib in al-Mufradat. What is meant by obedience with submissiveness is ontological obedience (الطاعة التكوينية)—as suggested by the context—rather than legislative obedience (التشريعية), which might sometimes be violated.

This is because they are the angels, the jinn, and mankind. As for the angels, they possess nothing but the submissiveness of obedience. As for the jinn and mankind, they are obedient and submissive to the ontological causes and means (العلل والأسباب الكونية); whenever they attempt to nullify the effect of one ontological cause or means, they resort to another ontological cause and means. Furthermore, their knowledge and will, like their entire free choice, are among the ontological causes; thus, nothing occurs except what Allah wills—that is, that whose causes have been completed in external reality—and nothing of what they will is realized except that which He has permitted and willed. Thus, He is the Owner of them and of what they possess.

Ayahs

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٧)
It is He who originates the creation, and then He will bring it back—and that is more simple for Him. His is the loftiest description in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (27)
ضَرَبَ لَكُم مَّثَلًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۖ هَل لَّكُم مِّن مَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُم مِّن شُرَكَآءَ فِى مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ فَأَنتُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ تَخَافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٢٨)
He draws for you an example from yourselves: Do you have among your slaves any partners [who may share] in what We have provided you, so that you are equal in its respect, and you revere them as you revere one another? Thus do We elaborate the signs for people who exercise their reason. (28)
بَلِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۖ فَمَن يَهْدِى مَنْ أَضَلَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٩)
Indeed, the wrongdoers follow their own desires without any knowledge. So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray? They will have no helpers. (29)
فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا ۚ فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى فَطَرَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٠)
So set your heart as a person of pure faith on this religion, the original nature endowed by Allah according to which He originated mankind (There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know.) (30)
۞ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ وَٱتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٣١)
—turning to Him in penitence, and be wary of Him, and maintain the prayer, and do not be one of the polytheists (31)
مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ شِيَعًا ۖ كُلُّ حِزْبٍۭ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ (٣٢)
—those who split up their religion and became sects: each faction boasting about what it possessed. (32)
وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلنَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا۟ رَبَّهُم مُّنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذَآ أَذَاقَهُم مِّنْهُ رَحْمَةً إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٣٣)
When distress befalls people, they supplicate their Lord, turning to Him in penitence. Then, when He lets them taste His mercy, behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord, (33)
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ۚ فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٤)
being ungrateful toward what We have given them. So let them enjoy. Soon they will know! (34)
أَمْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ يَتَكَلَّمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يُشْرِكُونَ (٣٥)
Have We sent down to them any authority which might assert what they associate with Him? (35)
وَإِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلنَّاسَ رَحْمَةً فَرِحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ إِذَا هُمْ يَقْنَطُونَ (٣٦)
When We let people taste [Our] mercy, they boast about it; but should an ill visit them because of what their hands have sent ahead, behold, they become despondent! (36)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٧)
Do they not see that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (37)
فَـَٔاتِ ذَا ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُۥ وَٱلْمِسْكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٣٨)
Give the relative his due, and the needy and the traveller [as well]. That is better for those who seek Allah’s pleasure, and it is they who are the felicitous. (38)
وَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَا۟ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُوا۟ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَوٰةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ ٱللَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُضْعِفُونَ (٣٩)
What you give in usury in order that it may increase people’s wealth does not increase with Allah. But what you pay as zakat seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will be given a manifold increase. (39)

(Commentary)

When the argumentation for monotheism (الوحدانية) and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and restoration of life after death — proceeded by way of enumerating the signs (الآيات) indicating them, starting from His saying: وَمِنْ آياتِهِ (And of His signs...) until His saying: وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth...), which pertains to the attributes of action (صفات الفعل), the discourse shifted from the style of arguing through signs to the style of arguing through His active attributes. He presented these until the end of the Surah in four sections, mentioning in each section something of the attributes of action that necessitate monotheism and the Return. These are His sayings: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ (It is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back...), اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ (It is Allah who created you and then provided for you...), اللهُ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ (It is Allah who sends the winds...), and اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ ضَعْفٍ (It is Allah who created you from [a state of] frailty...).

The first section did not begin with the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) — the name "Allah" — as the other sections did, because it had already been mentioned in the verse immediately preceding and connected to it, namely His saying: وَلَهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلٌّ لَهُ قانِتُونَ (To Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth; all are obedient to Him), which serves as an intermediate realm (البرزخ) — a transitional link — between the two contexts. Thus, His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ (It is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back...) is a section presented in the form of a continuous connection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (It is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back, and that is easier for Him...) to the end of the verse. The origination of creation (بدء الخلق) is its initial production without a prior model, and the restoration (الإعادة) is production after production.

Regarding His saying: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (and that is easier for Him), the first pronoun refers to the restoration (الإعادة) understood from His saying: يُعِيدُهُ (He will bring it back), and the second pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, as is apparent from the context.

A problem has been raised regarding His saying: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (and that is easier for Him), which ostensibly indicates that restoration is easier and simpler for Him than the initial creation. This contradicts His power being absolute (مطلقة) and unlimited, for infinite power (القدرة اللامتناهية) does not vary in its relation to one thing over another; its relation to the difficult and the easy is identical. Thus, there is no place for a comparative adjective (اسم التفضيل) here.

Several responses have been given to this: First, that the pronoun in عَلَيْهِ (for him/it) refers to the creation (الخلق) rather than to Him, the Exalted. Thus, restoration is easier for the creation because it was preceded by the initiation, which makes an act easier for the doer once it has been realized once or more, unlike the initiation which is not preceded by an act. Thus, initiation is naturally more difficult relative to restoration, and restoration is the opposite. The meaning would then be: restoration is easier than initiation relative to the creation, and if it is so relative to the creation, then what of the Creator?

The flaw in this is that referring the pronoun to the creation is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse.

Second, that the comparative form (أفعل) here is stripped of the meaning of comparison; thus, "easier for Him" (أهون عليه) means "easy for Him" (هين عليه), similar to His saying: ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ (what is with Allah is better than diversion).

The flaw in this is that it is an arbitrary claim with no evidence.

Third, that the comparison applies to the restoration in itself relative to the initial production, not relative to Him, the Exalted. There is no harm in a comparison occurring between one of His acts and another, as in His saying, the Exalted: لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ (The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind) [Al-Mu’min: 57].

This is what is understood from the words of al-Zamakhshari when he says: "If you ask: Why was the restoration magnified in His saying: ثُمَّ إِذا دَعاكُمْ (then, when He calls you...), as if it were preferred over the standing of the heavens and the earth by His command, and then deemed easy after that? I say: The restoration in itself is great, but it was deemed easy in comparison to the initial production." End quote.

The flaw in this is that the qualification of the description with His saying: عَلَيْهِ (for Him) is the truest witness that the comparison occurring between restoration and production is only relative to Him, the Exalted, not between the restoration and production themselves. Thus, the problem remains as it was.

Fourth, that the comparison is only in view of the principles circulating among people and the standards followed by them, not in view of the matter in itself. This is because they see that the repetition of an occurrence, even once, necessitates its ease for the doer relative to an act not preceded by its like. It is as if it were said: restoration is easier for Him in view of your followed intellectual principles; otherwise, production and restoration are identical relative to Him, the Exalted.

The flaw in this is that while it is a correct meaning in itself, the issue lies in deriving it from the wording, and there is no evidence for it from the phrasing of the verse.

Fifth, what was also mentioned in al-Kashshaf: He said: "Another perspective is that production is of the nature of a favor (التفضل) in which the doer has the choice to do it or not, while restoration is of the nature of an obligation (الواجب) which He must perform because it is for the recompense of deeds, and their recompense is obligatory. Acts are either impossible (محال) — and the impossible is inherently precluded and outside the scope of power — or they are something from which a wise agent is diverted by a deterrent, which is the loathsome (القبيح), and it is a counterpart to the impossible because the deterrent prevents the existence of the act just as impossibility prevents it. Or they are a favor, which is a state in between where the doer may do it or not. Or they are an obligation that must be done and there is no way to neglect it.

Thus, the obligatory was the furthest of acts from preclusion and the closest to occurrence. Since restoration was of the nature of the obligatory, it was the furthest of acts from preclusion, and being the furthest from preclusion, it was the most entering into feasibility and ease; thus it was easier than them, and if it was easier than them, it was easier than production." End quote.

The flaw in this is, firstly: it is built upon things being realized through priority (الأولوية) rather than necessity (الوجوب), and the falsity of this has been established in its proper place.

Secondly: the closeness and distance he mentioned are purely mental conceptualizations, while ease and difficulty are existential (وجوديان) attributes that characterize the existence of a thing insofar as it issues from its agent who brings it into being. An existential attribute is not built upon a mental consideration (الاعتبار العقلي).

Thirdly: production, like restoration, is built upon a benefit (المصلحة), which is the purpose (الغاية). As long as production does not have a benefit necessitating it, it is not realized, just as restoration is. Thus, they are identical in their closeness and distance from preclusion, as has been said.

Fourthly: the implication of this perspective is that restoration is easier than production in view of themselves, so it returns in reality to the third perspective, and what was directed at that is directed at this.

What ought to be said is that the sentence, namely His saying: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (and that is easier for Him), is justified by His saying after it: وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (To Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise). This is the proof establishing His saying: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ.

What is understood from His saying: وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى (To Him belongs the highest attribute...) etc., is that every attribute of perfection (وصف كمالي) by which something in the heavens and the earth is exemplified — such as life, power, knowledge, sovereignty, generosity, nobility, greatness, majesty, and others — belongs to Allah, the Glorified, in its highest degree and most elevated rank compared to those limited existents, as He said: وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى (To Allah belong the Best Names) [Al-A’raf: 180].

This is because every attribute of perfection characterized by something in the heavens and the earth has, in itself, its opposite; for it is something Allah bestowed upon it, while it is inherently devoid of it. The living among them is dead in its essence, and the powerful among them is weak in its essence. Therefore, the attribute in them was limited (محدود) and restricted (مقيد) to one thing over another and one state over another. Thus, knowledge in them, for example, is not absolute and unlimited, but limited and mixed with ignorance of what lies beyond it. Likewise are life, power, sovereignty, greatness, and others.

Allah, the Glorified, is the Bestower of these attributes from His grace, and what He possesses of the meaning of these attributes is absolute (مطلق), unlimited, and pure (صرف), unmixed. There is no ignorance to oppose His knowledge, nor death to oppose His life, and so on. Thus, He, the Glorified, possesses of every attribute by which the heavenly and earthly existents are characterized — which are attributes that are neither pure nor absolute — that which is the highest, meaning the absolute and the pure.

Every attribute found in Him, the Exalted, and in other creatures, that which is in Him is the highest and most excellent, while that which is in others is inferior relative to what is with Him.

Since restoration is characterized by ease (الهون) when compared to production in what is with the creation, it is for Him, the Exalted, "easier" (أهون), meaning pure ease unmixed with difficulty or hardship, unlike what is with us, the assembly of creation. This does not necessitate that there be difficulty or hardship for Him, the Exalted, in production, because hardship and difficulty in an act follow the power of the agent inversely: whenever power decreases, hardship increases, and whenever it increases, hardship decreases, until if power is infinite, hardship vanishes entirely. His power, the Exalted, is infinite, so no act is difficult for Him at all. This is what is understood from His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Indeed Allah has power over all things); for if power can relate to everything, it can only be infinite. Understand this.

His saying: وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth): it was previously mentioned that this is in the position of proof relative to His saying: وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (and that is easier for Him). Its summary is that every attribute of perfection by which anything in the heavens and the earth is characterized, whether of beauty (جمال) or majesty (جلال), belongs to Allah, the Glorified, in its highest form — that is, the absolute without restriction, the pure without impurity, and the unalloyed without mixture.

His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise) is in the position of justification relative to His saying: وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى (To Him belongs the highest attribute...) etc. That is, He, the Exalted, is All-mighty (عزيز) — possessing everything that others lack, and being such that nothing can resist Him — and All-wise (حكيم), whose act is never subject to frailty. If any of His attributes were not a "highest attribute" compared to what is with other contingent beings (الممكنات), it would be limited, not absolute, and mixed, not pure, not free from deficiency and shortcoming. That shortcoming would then humble Him, so He would not be All-mighty in the absolute sense, and that deficiency would create a breach and frailty in His act, so He would not be All-wise in the absolute sense.

His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ لَكُمْ مَثَلاً مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ هَلْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ مِنْ شُرَكاءَ فِي ما رَزَقْناكُمْ فَأَنْتُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ تَخافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (He draws for you a similitude from your own selves: Do you have among those whom your right hands possess any partners in what We have provided you, so that you are equal in that respect, and you fear them as you fear each other?...) etc. The word مِنْ (from) in His saying: مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ (from your own selves) indicates the starting point (ابتداء الغاية), meaning He struck for you a parable taken from yourselves, extracted from the conditions you have. His saying: هَلْ لَكُمْ (Do you have...) is the beginning of the parable struck, and the interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). The word ما in مِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ (from what [your right hands] possess) refers to the type, meaning from the type of those whom your right hands possess of male and female slaves. The مِنْ in مِنْ شُرَكاءَ (any partners) is extra (زائدة) — used for emphasis — and it is the subject (مبتدأ). His saying: فَأَنْتُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ (so that you are equal in that respect) is a consequence (تفريع) of the partnership. The word فَأَنْتُمْ (so you) is an address encompassing both the owners and the owned by way of predominance (التغليب). His saying: تَخافُونَهُمْ كَخِيفَتِكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ (you fear them as you fear each other) means you fear the slave partners that you might act independently in disposing of the shared wealth without their permission and consent, just as you fear your free partners.

This is a parable Allah struck to clarify the falsehood of what they claim — that among what Allah created are partners in divinity (الألوهية) and lordship (الربوبية). He presented the parable in the form of a denigrating interrogation: Is there found among your owned slaves, male or female, those who are partners with you in the wealth We have provided you — while they are your slaves whom you own along with what is in their hands — such that you fear them regarding the disposal of your wealth without their permission and consent, just as you fear free partners of your own kind?!

That never happens, and it is not permissible for a slave to be a partner to his master in his wealth. If it is not permissible, then how can it be permissible for some of what Allah created, like the angels and the jinn — who are His owned servants — to be partners to Him in what He owns of His creatures, and to be gods and lords besides Him?

Then He completed the speech by saying: كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who exercise their reason), which serves as a preparation for the speech that follows.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ اتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ فَمَنْ يَهْدِي مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللهُ وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ (Rather the wrongdoers follow their own desires without any knowledge.
