So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray? They will have no helpers). This is a transition (إضراب) from what is understood from the end of the previous verse. The implied meaning is: these polytheists did not build their polytheism (شركهم) upon reason (التعقل), but rather followed their desires in that without knowledge.

The apparent requirement was to say: "Rather those who associated partners (أشركوا) followed," but He replaced it with His saying: بَلِ اتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا (Rather the wrongdoers follow), describing them with wrongdoing (الظلم) to justify what He would later describe as misguidance in His saying: فَمَنْ يَهْدِي مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللهُ (So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray?). Thus, wrongdoing entails divine misguidance (الإضلال الإلهي). He, the Exalted, said: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ وَيُضِلُّ اللهُ الظَّالِمِينَ وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ (Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes) [Ibrahim: 27].

So His saying: فَمَنْ يَهْدِي مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللهُ (So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray?) is a denigrating interrogation whose meaning is to cause despair regarding the blessing of guidance (الهداية) for the polytheists who follow their desires despite the truth appearing to them, due to their wrongdoing which necessitates their misguidance. It has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ (Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people).

His saying: وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ (They will have no helpers) is a negation of their salvation through the assistance of helpers from others, after they failed to attain salvation from misguidance and its consequences from themselves due to Allah leading them astray. The negation of the plural (الجمع) is evidence that others have helpers, such as the intercessors (الشفعاء).

The claim of some that the negation of "helpers" (الناصرين) for them means that not a single one of them has a single helper — based on the famous rule of matching plural with plural — is not consistently applicable.

The meaning of the verse is: Rather, those who did wrong through their polytheism followed their desires without knowledge or reason, so Allah led them astray because of their wrongdoing, and there is no guide to guide them, nor do they have helpers to assist them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know). The speech is a consequence (متفرع) of what was obtained from the previous verses establishing the Origin (المبدأ) — the first cause of existence — and the Return (المعاد). That is: if it is established that creation and governance belong to Allah alone without partner, and He will resurrect and call to account, and there is no salvation for one who turns away from Him and turns to another, then set your face toward the religion and adhere to it, for it is the religion to which the divine creation calls.

It is said: the speech is a consequence of the meaning of consolation understood from the context of the previous explanation, which indicates what the truth is and that the polytheists, due to their wrongdoing, followed desires and turned away from correct reasoning, so Allah led them astray and did not permit a helper to assist them with guidance, nor a savior to save them from misguidance — neither you nor anyone else. So despair of them and concern yourself with your own soul and those who follow you of the believers, and set your face and those who follow you toward the religion.

His saying: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ (So set your face toward the religion): what is meant by setting the face toward the religion is turning toward it with focus without any heedlessness, like one who turns toward a thing by restricting his gaze to it such that he does not turn away from it to the right or left. It is apparent that the "alif-lam" in "the religion" (الدين) is for reference (للعهد), and what is meant by it is Islam.

His saying: حَنِيفاً (as a person of pure faith) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the subject of "set" (أقم). It has been allowed that it be a qualifier for "the religion" or for "the face," but the first is more apparent and more suited to the context. الحنف (al-hanaf) is the leaning of the two feet toward the center, and what is meant by it is moderation (الاعتدال).

His saying: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها (the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind): the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is a noun of type from "al-fatr" (الفطر) meaning origination and innovation. فِطْرَتَ اللهِ is in the accusative case for incitement (الإغراء), meaning: adhere to the primordial nature. In it is an indication that this religion, toward which the face must be set, is that which the creation calls out for and to which the divine primordial nature guides, for which there is no alteration.

That is because religion is nothing but the law of life (سنة الحياة) and the path that man must traverse to be happy in his life. Man has no goal to follow except happiness (السعادة), and every species of creation has been guided to its happiness — which is the object of its life — by its primordial nature and the type of its creation, and it has been equipped in its existence with what suits its goal. He, the Exalted, said: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى (Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it) [Taha: 50], and He said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى (He who created and proportioned, and who determined and guided) [Al-A’la: 2-3].

Thus, man, like all other created species, is fashioned with a primordial nature that guides him to complete his deficiencies and fulfill his needs, and calls out to him regarding what benefits him and what harms him in his life. He, the Exalted, said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها (By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness) [Ash-Shams: 7-8]. He is, moreover, equipped with that by which what he must aim for of action is completed for him. He, the Exalted, said: ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ (Then He made the way easy for him) [Abasa: 20].

So man has a specific primordial nature that guides him to a specific law in life and a designated path with a defined goal, which he has no choice but to traverse specifically. This is His saying: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها (the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind). Man living in this world is but a single species; what benefits him and what harms him does not differ in view of this structure composed of spirit and body. Thus, for man, insofar as he is man, there is only one happiness and one wretchedness. It is necessary then that there be, for his action, a single, stable law to which a single, stable guide leads him.

Let that guide be the primordial nature and the type of creation. Therefore, He followed His saying: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها with His saying: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ (There is no altering Allah’s creation).

If human happiness differed according to the difference of individuals, a single righteous society that guarantees the happiness of the socialized individuals would not be formed. If happiness differed according to the regions in which different nations live — in the sense that the sole basis for the social law, i.e., religion, was what the judgment of the region necessitates — then man would be different species according to the regions. If happiness differed according to the difference of times — in the sense that eras and centuries were the sole basis for the religious law — the nature of each century and generation would differ from those they inherited from their fathers or left to their sons, and human society would not proceed on a course of perfection (التكامل), nor would humanity be heading from deficiency toward perfection, since deficiency and perfection are not realized except with a stable, preserved common matter between them.

What is meant by this is not to deny that the difference of individuals, places, or times has some influence in the organization of the religious law in general, but rather to establish that the basis for the religious law is the human structure, which is a single, stable reality shared among individuals. Thus, for humanity, there is a single law, stable by the stability of its basis, which is man, and it is that which manages the wheel of humanity along with the partial laws that accompany it, which differ according to individuals, places, or times.

This is what He points to in His saying afterward: ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (that is the upright religion, but most people do not know). We shall further clarify this matter in an independent discussion, Allah willing.

The commentators have other scattered opinions regarding the vocabulary and meaning of the verse: Among them: that what is meant by setting the face is the rectifying of action, for the face is what one turns toward, which is the action, and setting it is rectifying it.

The flaw in this: is that the "face" of an action is its intended goal, which is other than the action itself, and what is in the verse is فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ (set your face), and He did not say "set the face of your action."

Among them: that فِطْرَتَ اللهِ (the primordial nature of Allah) is in the accusative case by implying "I mean" (أعني), and the primordial nature is the creed (الملة). The meaning would be: remain firm and continue the uprightness for the religion, I mean the creed upon which Allah created mankind; there is no altering Allah’s creation.

The flaw in this: is that it is built upon a difference between what is meant by the primordial nature — which is the creed — and فَطَرَ النَّاسَ (He originated mankind) — which is the creation. Such a separation is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. If فَطَرَ النَّاسَ were taken to mean "subjecting to religion," i.e., compelling toward the religion which is monotheism (التوحيد), then His saying: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ (There is no altering Allah’s creation) would remain unsuited to what precedes it.

Moreover, there is another contradiction to the apparent meaning, which is taking "the religion" to mean monotheism. If "the religion" were taken to mean Islam or the entirety of the religion, and the primordial nature were kept at its primary understood meaning — which is the creation — the implication of "I mean" would not be sound, for the religion in this sense is other than the primordial nature in the sense of creation.

Among them: that فِطْرَتَ is a substitute (بدل) for حَنِيفاً, and the primordial nature means the creed. The same objections as the previous one apply to this.

Among them: that فِطْرَتَ is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted, implied verb. The implication would be: Allah originated an origination (فطر الله فطرة) upon which He originated mankind. Its falsity is too obvious to need explanation.

Among them: that its meaning is: follow of the religion that which the primordial nature of Allah has pointed out to you, which is what the beginning of His creation of things pointed out to you, because He created them, composed them, and fashioned them in a way that indicates they have a Maker (صانع) who is All-powerful, All-knowing, Living, Eternal, One, who resembles nothing and nothing resembles Him.

The flaw in this: is that it is built upon فِطْرَتَ being in the accusative case by implying "follow" (اتبع). This was mentioned by Abu al-Sa'ud and before him Abu Muslim the commentator. Thus, the meaning of following the primordial nature would be following the indication of the primordial nature in the sense of creation, and the meaning of not altering the creation would be its lack of change in indicating the Maker with His noble attributes. This is close to the meaning we presented for the verse by taking فِطْرَتَ as incitement, but it remains that the verse is general and there is no evidence for restricting it to monotheism.

Among them: that the "la" (no) in His saying: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ (There is no altering Allah’s creation) signifies a prohibition (النهي), meaning: do not alter Allah’s creation, i.e., His religion which you were commanded to adhere to; or: do not alter Allah’s creation by denying its indication of monotheism. From this is what is attributed to Ibn Abbas, that what is meant by it is the prohibition of castration.

The flaw in this: is that there is no evidence for taking "creation" (الخلق) to mean "religion," nor is there any necessity to call the turning away from the indication of creation or its denial an "altering of Allah’s creation." As for what is attributed to Ibn Abbas, its falsity is apparent.

Among them: what al-Razi mentioned in al-Tafsir al-Kabir. He said: "It is possible to say: Allah created the creation for His worship, and they are all His servants; there is no altering Allah’s creation, meaning their being servants is not like an owned slave being a servant to a human, for he is transferred from him to another and leaves his ownership through manumission. Rather, there is no exit for the creation from worship (العبادة) and servitude (العبودية). This is to clarify the falsity of the saying of those who say: worship is for the attainment of perfection, and the servant becomes perfect through worship so no obligation remains upon him; and the saying of the polytheists: the deficient is not fit for the worship of Allah, but rather man is a servant of the stars and the stars are servants of Allah; and the saying of the Christians that Allah dwelt in Jesus and he became a god. So He said: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ; rather, they are all servants with no exit for them from that." End quote.

The flaw in this: is that it is a sophistry between ontological (تكوينيين) ownership and worship and legislative (تشريعيين) ownership and worship. His ownership, the Exalted, which does not accept transfer or nullification, is an ontological ownership in the sense of the existence of things subsisting through Him, the Exalted, and the worship corresponding to it is an ontological worship, which is the submission of the essences of things to Him, the Exalted, and it does not accept alteration or abandonment, as in His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ (There is not a thing but celebrates His praise) [Al-Isra: 44]. As for the religious worship which accepts alteration and abandonment, it is a legislative worship corresponding to the legislative ownership considered for Him, the Exalted. So understand this.

If His saying: لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ indicated the lack of alteration of ownership, worship, and servitude, it would indicate the ontological aspect of them. What is altered by those who claim the lifting of obligation from the perfect man, or by the worship of stars or the Messiah, is only the legislative aspect of them.

His saying, the Exalted: مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَلا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (turning to Him in penitence, and be wary of Him, and maintain the prayer, and do not be among the polytheists). This is a generalization of the address after its specification to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), similar to His saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّساءَ (O Prophet! When you [people] divorce women...) [At-Talaq: 1] and His saying: فَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَمَنْ تابَ مَعَكَ وَلا تَطْغَوْا (So be steadfast, just as you have been commanded—[you] and whoever has turned [to Allah] with you—and do not unduly overstep) [Hud: 112].

The meaning thus resolves to something like our saying: "So set your face toward the religion as a person of pure faith, you and those with you, turning to Allah in penitence." Penitence (الإنابة) is returning through repentance (التوبة).

His saying: وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ (and be wary of Him, and maintain the prayer): godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure — according to the indication of the context, encompasses complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions, the Exalted. The specification of maintaining the prayer from among all other acts of worship for mention is due to the importance of its status, for it is the pillar of the religion.

His saying: وَلا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (and do not be among the polytheists): the discussion regarding its specification from among the prohibited things for mention is similar to the discussion regarding the prayer. Polytheism (الشرك) with Allah is the greatest of the destructive major sins, and He, the Exalted, has said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes) [An-Nisa: 48], among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكانُوا شِيَعاً كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ (of those who split up their religion and became sects, each party exulting in what it had). The "min" (of) is for clarification (للتبيين), and مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ etc., is a clarification of the polytheists. In it is their definition by their most specific attribute in their religion, which is their splitting up in their religion and their becoming sect after sect and party after party, each sect and party exulting and rejoicing in what they have of the religion. The reason for that is what He mentioned shortly before this in His saying: بَلِ اتَّبَعَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ فَمَنْ يَهْدِي مَنْ أَضَلَّ اللهُ وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ (Rather the wrongdoers follow their own desires without any knowledge. So who will guide those whom Allah has led astray? They will have no helpers). He clarified that they built their religion on the basis of desires (الأهواء), and that He does not guide them, nor is there any guide other than Him.

It is well known that the desire of the soul (هوى النفس) does not agree among souls, nor does it remain stable in one state without differing according to the difference of conditions. If it is the basis for the religion, it will not remain long without proceeding according to the course of desires and declining with their decline. There is no difference in that between the false religion and the true religion built on the basis of desire.

From here it becomes clear that the prohibition of splitting the word in religion is in reality a prohibition of building the religion on the basis of desire rather than reason. It is perhaps possible that the verse is a new sentence (استئناف), but that does not suit the context.

In the verse is a censure of the polytheists for the attribute they possess of splitting the word and sectarianism in religion.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا مَسَّ النَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا رَبَّهُمْ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذا أَذاقَهُمْ مِنْهُ رَحْمَةً إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (When distress befalls people, they supplicate their Lord, turning to Him in penitence; then, when He lets them taste a mercy from Him, behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord). The expression "touch" (المس) is used to indicate smallness and lightness, and the indefiniteness of "distress" (ضر) and "mercy" (رحمة) is also for that. The meaning is: when something of distress reaches people, even if a little, such as some illness, some poverty, or some hardship, they supplicate their Lord — who is Allah, the Glorified — while they are returning from others. Then, when Allah lets them taste a mercy from Him, behold, a group of these people ascribe partners to their Lord whom they were supplicating and whose lordship they were acknowledging, by taking peers and partners.

That is, they are ungrateful for the blessing by nature, even if they acknowledge it during distress. He took "a part of them" (فريقا منهم) for that because some of them are not like that.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَكْفُرُوا بِما آتَيْناهُمْ فَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (Let them be ungrateful for what We have given them! So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know!). This is a threat (تهديد) to those polytheists upon the tasting of mercy. The "lam" in لِيَكْفُرُوا (Let them be ungrateful) is for the third-person command (الأمر الغائب). His saying: فَتَمَتَّعُوا (So enjoy yourselves) is a consequence of the previous one and is another command, and both commands are for threat. The shift (الالتفات) from the third-person command to the second-person command is due to the eruption of grief and wrath at their negligence toward Allah and their belittling of His command; for it reached such a point with them that they would entreat during distress and disbelieve when it is removed.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطاناً فَهُوَ يَتَكَلَّمُ بِما كانُوا بِهِ يُشْرِكُونَ (Have We sent down to them any authority which speaks of what they have been ascribing as partners to Him?). The "am" (or) is disjunctive (منقطعة). What is meant by "sending down" (الإنزال) is informing or teaching metaphorically. السلطان (authority) is the proof (البرهان). What is meant by "speaking" (التكلم) is indicating metaphorically. So the meaning is: rather, have We informed them of a proof that indicates what they were ascribing as partners, or their polytheism?

It is possible that what is meant by "authority" (السلطان) is the possessor of authority, which is the angel (الملك), so there is no metaphor in "sending down" and "speaking." The meaning would be: rather, have We sent down to them an angel who speaks of what they were ascribing as partners, or their polytheism?

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا أَذَقْنَا النَّاسَ رَحْمَةً فَرِحُوا بِها وَإِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ إِذا هُمْ يَقْنَطُونَ (When We let people taste a mercy, they exult in it; but if an ill visits them because of what their hands have sent ahead, behold, they become despondent). "Tasting" (الإذاقة), like "touching," indicates a small and slight attainment. القنوط (al-qunut) is despair (اليأس).

The first "idha" (when) is conditional (شرطية) and the second is for surprise (فجائية). The contrast between "idha" in the tasting of mercy and "in" (if) in the visiting of an ill is because mercy is abundant and certain, while the ill is little and probable. The attribution of mercy to Him, the Exalted, rather than the ill, is because mercy is existential, bestowed by Him, the Exalted, while the ill is privative, being the lack of bestowal. Therefore, He justified it by His saying: بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ (because of what their hands have sent ahead). In the justification of the ill by that and the lack of justification on the side of mercy by anything is an indication that mercy is a favor.

The expression regarding mercy with His saying: فَرِحُوا (they exult) and regarding the ill with His saying: إِذا هُمْ يَقْنَطُونَ (behold, they become despondent) is to indicate the occurrence of despair when it was not expected; for mercy and the ill are in the hand of Allah, and mercy is vast. For this reason, He expressed it with the present tense (المضارع) indicating the current state to represent their condition.

What is meant by the verse is to clarify that people's vision does not go beyond the outward appearance of what they witness of blessing and affliction. When they find [blessing], they exult in it without reflecting or reasoning that the matter is in the hand of another and by the will of their Lord; if He did not will it, it would not be. When they lose [it], they despair as if that were not by the permission of their Lord; if He did not will it, He would not permit it and would open the door of blessing. Thus, they are superficial and literalist.

By this, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between this verse and His previous saying: وَإِذا مَسَّ النَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا رَبَّهُمْ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ (When distress befalls people, they supplicate their Lord, turning to Him in penitence...). This is because the import of this verse is that their understandings are superficial: when they find, they exult, and when they lose, they despair. The import of that verse is that when they find, they exult, and when they lose, they supplicate Allah while they are despairing of the thing and its causes, turning and returning to Allah, the Glorified. So there is no contradiction.

It has perhaps been answered that what is meant by "people" in this verse is another group than the group meant by "people" in the previous verse. Even if their identity were assumed, what was mentioned of their supplication would be in one state and their despair in another state.

It was also answered that the supplication is verbal, following habit, and does not contradict despair, which is a matter of the heart. You are aware of the weakness in both of these answers.

It was also answered that what is meant by their despair is their acting with the action of the despondent, such as the concern with gathering stores during days of high prices. In this, besides the lack of evidence for it, is that it does not suit the meaning of surprise in the despair.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللهَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (Do they not see that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith). This is a clarification of their error in hastening to exultation and despair upon the tasting of mercy and the visiting of an ill. For provision (الرزق), in its expansion and constriction, follows the will of Allah. Thus, man must know that the mercy he tasted and the ill that visited him are capable of vanishing by the will of Allah, the Glorified. There is no cause for exultation in what one is not secure from losing, nor for despair from what is hoped to vanish.

As for it being an apparent matter for man, certain as if he sees it, it is because the provision that man attains or earns is dependent for its existence upon thousands and thousands of causes and conditions. Man, who sees it for himself, is but one of those causes, and the cause he relies upon and finds comfort in is but some of those causes. All causes end with Him, the Glorified; He is the one who gives and withholds, and He is the one who expands and tightens, i.e., widens and narrows. The rest is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: فَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبى حَقَّهُ وَالْمِسْكِينَ وَابْنَ السَّبِيلِ (So give the relative his due, and the needy and the traveller...) etc. ذو القربى (the relative) is the possessor of kinship from the womb-relatives. المسكين (the needy) is in a worse state than the poor (الفقير). ابن السبيل (the traveller) is the wayfarer in need. The addition of "due" (الحق) to the pronoun indicates that the relative has a fixed right. The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The apparent meaning of the verse, with the surrounding context, is that what is meant by it is the one-fifth (الخمس) — the mandatory religious tax of one-fifth of certain types of income — and the obligation is upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), followed by others who are obligated with the one-fifth. The kinship, in any case, is the kinship of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as in the verse of the one-fifth. All of this is on the assumption that the verse is Medinan (مدنية); as for the assumption that it is Meccan (مكية) like the other verses of the Surah, what is meant is absolute kindness to the relative, the needy, and the traveller.

Due to the generality of the verse, He mentioned its beautiful effect in a general way, saying: ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ (That is better for those who seek Allah’s pleasure, and it is they who are the felicitous).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ رِباً لِيَرْبُوَا فِي أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ فَلا يَرْبُوا عِنْدَ اللهِ ، وَما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ زَكاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ (What you give as usury in order that it may increase people’s wealth does not increase with Allah. But what you give as charity seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase). Usury (الربا) is the increase of wealth. His saying: لِيَرْبُوَا (in order that it may increase) etc., points to the reason for the naming. What is meant is that the wealth you give to people so that it may increase in their wealth — without seeking the pleasure of Allah, by the evidence of mentioning the seeking of pleasure in contrast to it — does not increase and grow with Allah, meaning you will not be rewarded for it due to the lack of intending His pleasure.

His saying: وَما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ زَكاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ (But what you give as charity seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase): what is meant by charity (الزكاة) is absolute almsgiving (الصدقة), i.e., giving wealth for the sake of Allah without extravagance. المضعف (the one who receives a manifold increase) is the possessor of doubling. The meaning is: what you have given of wealth as alms seeking the pleasure of Allah, those are the ones for whom their wealth or their reward is doubled.

What is meant by usury and charity, by the evidence of the contrast and the surrounding indications, is the permissible usury (الربا الحلال), which is the gift without seeking proximity [to Allah], and almsgiving (الصدقة), which is giving wealth with the intention of proximity. All of this is on the assumption that the verse is Meccan; as for the assumption that it is Medinan, what is meant by usury is the prohibited usury and by charity is the obligatory charity (الزكاة المفروضة).

This verse and the one before it are more similar to the Medinan verses than the Meccan ones, and no weight is given to what is claimed of narration or transmitted consensus.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Uyun, narrated from Ubaydullah ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood among us as a speaker — and said at the end of his sermon: 'We are the word of godwariness (كلمة التقوى), the path of guidance (سبيل الهدى), the highest attribute (المثل الأعلى), the greatest proof (الحجة العظمى), and the firmest handle (العروة الوثقى).'" The tradition continues.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ لَكُمْ مَثَلاً مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ (He draws for you a similitude from your own selves...) the verse — that the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول) was that Quraysh used to perform the pilgrimage to the House according to the pilgrimage of Ibrahim (عليه السلام) — and they would recite his talbiyah: "Labbayk Allahumma labbayk, labbayk la sharika laka labbayk — indeed praise and blessing are Yours, and the kingdom, no partner have You."

Then Iblis came to them in the form of an old man and changed their talbiyah — to the saying: "Labbayk Allahumma, no partner have You except a partner who is Yours; You own him and what he owns." So Quraysh used to recite this talbiyah until the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent — and he denounced that to them and said: "It is polytheism (شرك)."

So Allah (عز وجل) sent down: ضَرَبَ لَكُمْ مَثَلاً مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ ـ هَلْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ مِنْ شُرَكاءَ فِي ما رَزَقْناكُمْ ـ فَأَنْتُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ (He draws for you a similitude from your own selves: Do you have among those whom your right hands possess any partners in what We have provided you, so that you are equal in that respect?...) meaning: do you yourselves accept, regarding what you own, that you have a partner in it? Then how do you accept to make for Me a partner in what I own?

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً (So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith), he said: "It is the Mastership (الولاية) — the divinely appointed authority and leadership of the Ahl al-Bayt."

In it, with its chain of narration from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), he said: I said: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها (the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind). He said: "It is monotheism (التوحيد)."

I say: It was also narrated from al-Halabi and Zurarah from him (عليه السلام), and al-Saduq narrated it in al-Tawhid from al-Ala ibn Fudayl, Zurarah, and Bukayr from him (عليه السلام).

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Isma'il al-Ju'fi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), he said: "The law (شريعة) of Nuh (عليه السلام) was — that Allah be worshipped with monotheism and sincerity and the casting off of peers, and it is the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which He originated mankind."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from al-Haytham al-Rummani from al-Rida from his father from his grandfather from his father Muhammad ibn Ali (عليه السلام): regarding His saying (عز وجل): فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها, he said: "It is 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, Ali the Commander of the Faithful is the Friend of Allah' — up to here is monotheism (التوحيد)."

I say: This meaning was narrated in Basa'ir al-Darajat from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), and it was narrated in al-Tawhid from Abd al-Rahman, the client of Abu Ja'far, from him (عليه السلام).

The meaning of the primordial nature being the three testimonies (الشهادات الثلاث) is that man is fashioned to acknowledge Allah without partner by what he finds of need for causes that are in need of what is beyond them, which is monotheism; and by what he finds of deficiency that necessitates a religion he adheres to in order to complete him, which is prophethood (النبوة); and by what he finds of need to enter into the mastership of Allah by organizing action according to the religion, which is the Mastership (الولاية), and the one who opened it in Islam is Ali (عليه السلام). It does not mean that every human, even the first human, adheres by his primordial nature specifically to the three testimonies.

To this resolves the meaning of the previous narration that it is the Mastership, for it necessitates monotheism and prophethood. Likewise, what passed of his interpreting the primordial nature as monotheism; for monotheism is the saying of the oneness of Allah, the Exalted, who encompasses the attributes of perfection that necessitate the Return, prophethood, and Mastership. Thus, the outcome in interpreting it as the three testimonies, monotheism, and Mastership is one.

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, he said: I asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah (عز وجل) فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها. He said: "He originated them upon the knowledge that He is their Lord — and were it not for that, they would not know when asked who is their Lord and who is their Provider."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Nu'aym al-Sahhaf from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: He (عليه السلام) said: "Indeed Allah (عز وجل) created all people — upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which He originated them — they do not know faith through a law nor disbelief through denial — then Allah (عز وجل) sent the messengers calling the servants to faith in Him; so among them are those whom Allah guided and among them are those whom He did not guide."

I say: In this meaning are other narrations mentioned in the interpretation of His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً (Mankind were a single community) [Al-Baqarah: 213]. What is meant in them by the "primordial man" (الإنسان الفطري) is the simple man who lives according to the human primordial nature, who has not been corrupted by intellectual delusions and psychological desires; for he is in a state of potentiality close to actuality relative to the principles of true beliefs and the universals of divine laws, for he lives by the prompting and movement of his primordial nature and specific creation. As for being guided to the specific true beliefs and the details of divine laws, it depends on a specific divine guidance by way of prophethood from the second part of the Book.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Hammad ibn Amr al-Saffar, who said: I asked Qatadah about His saying, the Exalted: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها. He said: Anas ibn Malik narrated to me, saying: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها — he said: the religion of Allah."

In it, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Abu Hurayrah, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "There is no newborn except that he is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — then his parents make him a Jew, a Christian, or a Magian — just as the beast produces a complete beast; do you perceive in it any ear-cropped one?" Abu Hurayrah said: "Read if you wish: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها the verse."

I say: It was also narrated from Malik, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and its wording is: "Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature — then his parents make him a Jew or a Christian — just as the camels produce from a complete beast; do you perceive any ear-cropped one?"

It was also narrated in al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature, meaning upon the knowledge that Allah is his Creator." The tradition continues.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration from Umar, he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Do not strike your children for their crying — for their crying for four months is the testimony that there is no god but Allah, and for four months the sending of blessings upon the Prophet, and for four months the supplication for his parents."

I say: It is a subtle tradition, and its meaning is: the child in the first four months does not know anyone, but rather senses the need and seeks its removal through crying, and the remover of it is Allah, the Glorified; so he entreats Him and testifies to Him with oneness.

In the second four months, he knows from his parents a medium between him and the remover of his need without knowing them personally, and the medium between him and his Lord is the Prophet; so his crying is a request for mercy from his Lord for the Prophet so that it may reach him through his mediation.

In the third four months, he distinguishes his parents personally from others; so his crying is a supplication from him for them and a request for the flow of mercy through their path to him. In the tradition is the subtlest indication of how the emanation (الفيض) flows through the course of the media, so understand that.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبى حَقَّهُ (So give the relative his due): Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and others narrated: "That when this verse was sent down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — he gave Fatimah (عليه السلام) Fadak and handed it over to her — and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Ibrahim al-Yamani from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), he said: "Usury (الربا) is of two types: usury that is consumed and usury that is not consumed. As for that which is consumed, it is your gift to a man — seeking from him a reward better than it; that is the usury that is consumed, and it is the saying of Allah (عز وجل): وَما آتَيْتُمْ مِنْ رِباً لِيَرْبُوَا فِي أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ فَلا يَرْبُوا عِنْدَ اللهِ. As for that which is not consumed, it is that which Allah prohibited and threatened with the Fire for it."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Tahdhib from Ibrahim ibn Umar from him (عليه السلام), and in the Tafsir of al-Qummi from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from him (عليه السلام), and in al-Majma' as a disconnected narration (مرسلا) from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام).

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ (it is they who will receive a manifold increase), the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: "Allah ordained the prayer as a purification from pride, and the charity (الزكاة) as a cause for provision, and fasting as a trial for the sincerity of the creation, and the joining of kinship as a means for the growth of numbers."

In al-Faqih, a sermon by al-Zahra (عليه السلام) in which she said: "So Allah ordained faith as a cleansing from polytheism, and the prayer — as a purification from pride, and the charity as an increase in provision."

( A Discourse on the Meaning of the Religion Being Primordial, in Sections )

1 - If we contemplate these existing species that are formed and perfected gradually — whether they possess life and consciousness like the species of animals, or possess life only like the species of plants, or are dead without life like the other natural species — as it appears to us — we find each species among them proceeding in its existence according to a specific ontological course (سيرا تكوينيا) possessing different stages, some before others and some after others. The species enters each of them after passing through the one before it and before reaching what is after it, and it continues to seek perfection by traversing these stations until it ends at the last of them, which is the end of its perfection.

We find these ranks traversed by the movement of the species, each of them adhering to its specific place; it is neither advanced nor delayed from the beginning of the movement of the species in its existence until it ends at its perfection. Between them—

...an ontological bond (رابطة تكوينية) through which some levels are connected to others, such that it neither deviates nor moves to a place other than its own. From this, it is concluded that every species has an ontological goal (غاية تكوينية) toward which it directs itself from the very beginning of its existence until it reaches it.

For example, if a single walnut is settled in the earth in a manner that prepares it for growth—upon the convergence of the causes and conditions upon which growth depends, such as moisture, heat, and others—its kernel begins to grow, splits the shell, and starts increasing in its bodily dimensions. It continues to increase and grow until it reaches a point where it becomes a strong, green, fruit-bearing tree. Its state in this ontological journey does not falter; from the first moment of its existence, it is ontologically intending (قاصدا تكوينيا) toward its ontological goal, which is the stage of the complete, fruit-bearing tree.

Likewise, an individual of an animal species, such as a single sheep, for instance—we have no doubt that from its first formation as an embryo, it is directed toward its species-specific goal, which is the stage of the complete sheep possessing its particular characteristics. It does not stray from its specific ontological path to the path of another, nor does it ever forget its goal to travel toward the goal of another, such as the goal of an elephant or the goal of a walnut tree. Thus, every ontological species has a specific path in the perfection of existence (استكمال الوجود), possessing specific levels ordered one after another, ending at a stage that is the essence of the species' goal. It seeks this goal with an ontological seeking (طلبا تكوينيا) through its ontological movement, and the species is equipped in its existence with that which serves as the means for its movement and its attainment of its goal.

This ontological direction, by virtue of its attribution to Allah, is called general divine guidance (هداية عامة إلهية). As you have seen, it neither strays nor errs in directing every species through its ontological course and driving it toward its existential goal through gradual perfection (الاستكمال التدريجي) and by employing the powers and tools with which it was equipped to facilitate its journey to its goal. The Exalted said: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى "Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Ta-Ha: 50). And He said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى وَالَّذِي أَرْجَةَ الْمَرْعى فَجَعَلَهُ غُثاءً أَحْوى "Who created and fashioned, who determined and guided, who brought forth the pasture, then turned it into a blackened scud" (al-A'la: 2-5).

2 — The human species is no exception to the universality of the aforementioned rule—meaning the encompassment of general guidance over it. We know that the human sperm-drop, from the moment it begins its formation, is directed toward the stage of a sound, complete human being who possesses his effects and characteristics, having traversed in his journey the stages of the embryo, childhood, adolescence, youth, middle age, and old age.

However, man differs from all other animal and plant species and others—as far as we know—in one matter (1): due to the vastness of his ontological need (حاجته التكوينية) and the multiplicity of his existential deficiencies (نواقصه الوجودية), he is unable to complete his existential deficiencies and fulfill his vital needs alone. This means that a single human individual cannot achieve his human life while being solitary; rather, he requires a domestic association (اجتماع منزلي) and then a civil society (اجتماع مدني) in which he joins with others through marriage, cooperation, and mutual aid. Thus, everyone strives with all the powers they were equipped with for the sake of the whole, and then the product of their labor is divided among the whole, with each taking his portion according to his social weight.

You have already learned in previous discussions of this book that civilization is not natural (طبيعية) to man in the sense that it arises from his human nature primarily. Rather, he possesses a nature that seeks to employ (مستخدمة) others for the benefit of his own self whenever he finds a way to do so. He employs natural things, then various types of plants and animals, for the sake of his vital purposes. He is even bolder in employing an individual like himself or individuals like him; however, he finds that other individuals are like him in their inclinations, purposes, apparatuses, and powers. Thus, he is forced into peaceful coexistence (المسالمة) and to concede to them rights similar to what he sees for himself.

This conflict between interests ends with some participating with others in cooperative labor, then dividing the product of the labors among everyone, giving each what he deserves.

In any case, human society cannot be established nor can it flourish except through intellectual principles (أصول علمية) and social laws (قوانين اجتماعية) respected by all, and a guardian who protects them from being lost and implements them in society. At that point, life becomes pleasant for them and happiness (السعادة) dawns upon them.

As for the intellectual principles, they consist of his general knowledge of the reality of the realm of existence and the reality of man in terms of his beginning and end. Indeed, different creeds influence the specific traditions practiced in societies. Those who believe that man is purely material, having no life except the immediate life that is terminated by death, and that there is nothing in the abode of existence except material causes that come into being and pass away (الكائنة الفاسدة), organize the traditions of their society such that they lead them to sensory pleasures and material perfections, with nothing beyond that.

(1) Although animals in general possess something of a vital association, it is insignificant compared to [human] society and is not taken into account.

Those who believe in a Maker beyond matter, such as the idolaters, build their traditions and laws upon pleasing the gods so that they may grant them happiness in their worldly life. Those who believe in the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) build their lives on a foundation that brings them happiness in the worldly life and then in the eternal life after death. Thus, the forms of social life differ according to the differences in the foundational beliefs (الأصول الاعتقادية) regarding the reality of the world and man, who is a part of its parts.

As for the social laws and traditions, were it not for the existence of common laws and traditions respected by all or most of the members of society and accepted by them, the gathering would disperse and the society would dissolve.

These traditions and laws are universal practical propositions (قضايا كلية عملية), the forms of which are: "One must do such-and-such at such-a-time," or "It is forbidden," or "It is permissible." Whatever they may be, they are considered in action for the sake of goals that serve the interest of the association and the society, which result from them; these are called the benefits (مصالح) and harms (مفاسد) of actions.

3 — You have learned that man only attains the perfection and happiness destined for him by forming a righteous society in which righteous traditions and laws govern, ensuring his attainment of the happiness that befits him. This happiness consists of one or more ontological perfection-related matters (أمور كمالية تكوينية) that attach to the deficient human—who is also an ontological being—thereby making him a complete human in his species and perfect in his existence.

Thus, these traditions and laws—which are practical, conventional propositions (قضايا عملية اعتبارية)—stand between man's deficiency and his perfection, serving as a bridge between the two states. As you have seen, they follow the interests (المصالح) which are human perfection or perfections. These perfections are real matters (أمور حقيقية) that are congruent and harmonious with the deficiencies, which are the instances of man's real needs.

Therefore, it is man's real needs that have established these practical propositions and formulated these conventional laws (النواميس الاعتبارية). What is meant by "needs" are those things sought by the human soul through its inclinations and determinations, and which are affirmed by the intellect (العقل)—which is the sole power that distinguishes between the good and beneficial and the evil and harmful—excluding what is sought by the whims of the soul (الأهواء النفسانية) which the intellect does not affirm, for that is an animal perfection, not a human one.

Thus, the foundations of these traditions and laws must be the real needs—those which are needs according to reality, not according to the diagnosis of the soul's whims.

You have already learned that Fashioning and Origination (الصنع والإيجاد) have equipped every species—including man—with powers and tools through whose activity its needs are fulfilled and through which it travels the path of perfection. From this, it is concluded that the ontological apparatuses (الجهازات التكوينية) with which man was equipped have requirements (اقتضاءات) for the practical propositions called traditions and laws, through the practice of which man settles in the abode of his perfection. Examples include the traditions and laws related to nutrition, considered insofar as man is equipped with a digestive apparatus, and those related to marriage, insofar as man is equipped with a reproductive apparatus.

Thus, it becomes clear that it is necessary for the religion (الدين)—meaning the intellectual principles and the practical traditions and laws whose adoption and practice ensure man's true happiness—to be derived from the requirements of human creation (الخلقة الإنسانية), and for legislation (التشريع) to correspond with the primordial nature (الفطرة) and ontology (التكوين). This is the meaning of religion being "primordial" (فطري), and it is His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, the nature (fطرة) of Allah on which He fashioned mankind. There is no changing Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion" (ar-Rum: 30).

4 — You have understood the meaning of religion being primordial. Thus, Islam is called the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة) because the human primordial nature (الفطرة) requires it and guides toward it.

It is called Islam (إسلام) because it involves the servant's submission (تسليم) to the will of Allah, the Glorified, regarding him. The instance of the Will (الإرادة)—which is an attribute of action—gathers the causes composed of the specifics of man's creation and the surrounding requirements of the general cosmos to necessitate action or omission. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (Al 'Imran: 19).

It is called the religion of Allah (دين الله) because it is what Allah wills from His servants in terms of action or omission, according to the aforementioned meaning of Will.

It is called the way of Allah (سبيل الله) because it is the path that Allah willed for man to traverse so that it may lead him to his perfection and happiness. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً "Those who bar from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked" (al-A'raf: 45).

As for the fact that the true religion must be taken through the path of revelation (الوحي) and prophethood (النبوة) and that the intellect (العقل) is not sufficient for it, the explanation of this has already preceded in the discussions on prophethood and other sections of the book.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۖ هَلْ مِن شُرَكَآئِكُم مَّن يَفْعَلُ مِن ذَٰلِكُم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٤٠)
It is Allah who created you and then He provided for you, then He makes you die, then He will bring you to life. Is there anyone among your ‘partners’ who does anything of that kind? Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (40)
ظَهَرَ ٱلْفَسَادُ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِى ٱلنَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى عَمِلُوا۟ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤١)
Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of the doings of the people’s hands, that He may make them taste something of what they have done, so that they may come back. (41)
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّشْرِكِينَ (٤٢)
Say, ‘Travel over the land, and see how was the fate of those who were before [you], most of whom were polytheists.’ (42)
فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ ٱلْقَيِّمِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ (٤٣)
So set your heart on the upright religion, before there comes a day irrevocable from Allah. On that day they shall be split [into various groups]. (43)
مَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُۥ ۖ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِأَنفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ (٤٤)
Whoever is faithless shall face the consequences of his faithlessness, and those who act righteously only prepare for their own souls, (44)
لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٥)
so that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace. Indeed, He does not like the faithless. (45)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَن يُرْسِلَ ٱلرِّيَاحَ مُبَشِّرَٰتٍ وَلِيُذِيقَكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَلِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٤٦)
And of His signs is that He sends the winds as bearers of good news and to let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His bounty, and so that you may give [Him] thanks. (46)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ رُسُلًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِمْ فَجَآءُوهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ ۖ وَكَانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٧)
Certainly We sent apostles to their people before you, and they brought them manifest proofs. Then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful. (47)

(Commentary)

This is the second of the four sections in which He argues through His specific acts—or if you prefer, through the "Attributes of Act" (أسماء الأفعال)—for the invalidation of partners, the negation of their lordship (الربوبية) and divinity (الألوهية), and for the proof of the Resurrection (المعاد).

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ رَزَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ هَلْ مِنْ شُرَكَائِكُمْ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ مِنْ ذَلِكُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ "It is Allah who created you, then He provided for you, then He makes you die, then He will bring you to life. Is there anyone among your partners who does anything of that?" (30:40) etc. The Majestic Name (الله) is the subject (مبتدأ), and "the One who created you" (الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ) is its predicate (خبر). Similarly, His saying: "Is there anyone... who does" (مَنْ يَفْعَلُ) is a subject whose predicate is "among your partners" (مِنْ شُرَكَائِكُمْ), which precedes it. The interrogation here is one of denial (إنكاري). Other possibilities for the grammatical structure of the verse have also been mentioned.

The meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, is the One characterized by such-and-all attributes of divinity and lordship. Is there, then, among the deities whom you claim to be gods, anyone who does any of these things—meaning creation (الخلق), provision (الرزق), causing death (الإماتة), and bringing to life (الإحياء)? Since there is none among them who does any of that, then Allah, the Glorified, is your God and your Lord; there is no god but He.

Perhaps the reason for mentioning creation (الخلق) alongside provision (الرزق), and bringing to life (الإحياء) alongside causing death (الإماتة)—despite their repeated mention in previous arguments—is to indicate that provision is inseparable from creation. This means that some created things are called "provision" (رزق) in relation to others whose survival they sustain. Thus, provision is, in reality, a form of creation; so the One who creates the creation is the One who provides the provision.

Therefore, they have no right to say: "The Provider (الرازق), as well as the Giver of Life (المحيي) and the Bringer of Death (المميت), are some of our deities," as some of them might claim that the manager of the human world is one of the deities, and the manager of every affair of the world—whether good or evil—is another. However, they do not disagree that creation and origination (الإيجاد) are from Him, the Exalted, and that no one shares with Him in that. If this is conceded—and it is conceded that provision, for example, is a form of creation, and likewise all other affairs are inseparable from creation—then the matter returns to Him, the Exalted, just as creation does, and no role remains for their deities in any of these affairs.

Then, the Glorified declared His own transcendence (نزه سبحانه نفسه) above their polytheism, saying: سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners they ascribe [to Him]!" (30:40).

His saying, the Exalted: ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done, so that they may come back." (30:41). The verse, by its apparent wording, is general; it is not restricted to a particular time, place, or specific event. Thus, "land" (البر) and "sea" (البحر) are intended in their well-known meanings, encompassing the entire surface of the Earth.

"Corruption" (الفساد) refers to the manifest calamities and afflictions appearing in them, which affect various regions of the earth—such as earthquakes, the cessation of rain, droughts, contagious diseases, wars, raids, and the loss of security. In short, it includes everything that corrupts the sound order (النظام الصالح) prevailing in the terrestrial world, whether it stems from human choice or otherwise. All of these are manifest corruptions in the land or sea that disrupt the wholesome human life.

His saying: بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ "because of what the hands of the people have earned" means due to the deeds they perform, whether polytheism (الشرك) or disobedience (المعصية). It has already been mentioned in the commentary on His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth" (7:96), and also in the discussions on Prophethood in the second volume of this book, that there is a direct link between human actions and cosmic events; one is affected by the righteousness (صلاح) or corruption (فساد) of the other.

His saying: لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا "that He may let them taste some of what they have done"—the "lam" (ل) denotes the objective (للغاية). That is, what has appeared has appeared so that Allah may let them taste the evil consequence (وبال) of some of their wicked deeds; rather, so He may let them taste the very essence of what they have done, which has manifested in the form of an evil consequence. It was only "some" (بعض) of what they did because Allah, the Glorified, by His mercy, pardons some, as He said: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ "Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]." (42:30).

The verse looks toward the worldly consequence and the tasting of some of it, without regard for the consequence of deeds in the Hereafter. Thus, the claim that the meaning is "tasting the worldly consequence and delaying the otherworldly consequence until the Day of Resurrection" has no evidence. Perhaps this claimant assumed the implied meaning (تقدير) to be: "that He may let them taste some of the recompense for what they have done," whereas the implied meaning is: "that He may let them taste the recompense for some of what they have done." This is because what necessitates an implied noun—if anything does—is that what returns to them in the form of corruption is the recompense for their deeds, not the deeds themselves. Thus, what they are made to taste is the recompense for some of what they did, not some of the recompense for what they did.

His saying: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "so that they may come back" means He lets them taste what He lets them taste in the hope that they might return from their polytheism and disobedience to monotheism (التوحيد) and obedience (الطاعة).

The connection of this verse to the preceding one is that after He argued for monotheism in the previous verse and declared His transcendence above their polytheism, He pointed out in this verse the corruption on earth and the tasting of the evil consequences of sins that follow polytheism—which is itself a form of disobedience—explaining this in a general manner.

Exegetes have various strange interpretations for this verse. Some say "the land" (الأرض) refers to the land of Mecca. Others say "the land" (البر) refers to the deserts where no river flows, and "the sea" (البحر) refers to every village on the bank of a great river. Some say "the land" refers to the wilderness and tribal areas, while "the sea" refers to the coasts and cities (المدن) near the sea and rivers. Others say "the land" is the desert and "the sea" refers to fertile, green areas. Some say there is an omitted noun, the implied meaning being "in the land and the cities of the sea." Perhaps what led them to these claims is the report that the verse refers to the famine that occurred in Mecca following the prayer of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against the Quraysh when they persisted in their disbelief and stubbornness. They wanted to apply the verse to the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) and thus fell into such forced interpretations (التكلف).

Some also said that "corruption in the land" refers to the son of Adam killing his brother, and "in the sea" refers to the seizing of every ship by force. This is as you see [unfounded].

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُشْرِكِينَ "Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of those who were before [you]. Most of them were polytheists.’" (30:42). This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to order them to travel through the land and look at the ruins of those who came before, whose homes were destroyed, whose traces were erased, who perished to the last man, and whose lineage was cut off by various types of calamities and afflictions. Most of them were polytheists (مشركين), so Allah let them taste some of what they had done, so that those who take heed might learn and return to monotheism. Thus, the verse serves as a testimony to the content of the preceding verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ "So set your face toward the upright religion, before there comes a day from Allah which cannot be turned back. On that day they will be split into two groups." (30:43). This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded it; meaning, if polytheism and disbelief in the Truth are of such a nature and have an evil consequence that will overtake those who clothe themselves in them, then set your face toward the upright religion (الدين القيم) — the true and stable faith that maintains the correct order of life.

His saying: مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا مَرَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللهِ "before there comes a day from Allah which cannot be turned back" is connected to His saying: "So set" (فَأَقِمْ). The word "return" (المرد) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning "turning back" (الرد), and it carries the sense of "that which turns back" (الراد). The day that cannot be turned back from Allah is the Day of Resurrection.

His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ "On that day they will be split into two groups" is originally (يتصدعون). "Splitting" (التصدع) originally refers to the breaking apart of the pieces of a vessel, then it was used for any kind of separation. It is said that the meaning here is their separation on that day into Paradise and the Fire.

It is also said that it refers to the separation of people as individuals, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَراشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ "the day when mankind will be like scattered moths" (101:4). Each view has its merit, but perhaps the most apparent meaning is the distinction between the two groups, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ "Whoever is faithless, his faithlessness will be to his own detriment, and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls" (30:44). It appears that this is an explanation for His saying in the previous verse: "they will be split" (يَتَفَرَّقُونَ). His saying: "Whoever is faithless, his faithlessness will be to his own detriment" (مَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ) means the evil consequence of his disbelief—assuming an implied noun—or the very essence of his disbelief which will turn against him as a fire in which he will abide forever. This is one of the two groups.

His saying: وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ "and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls." To "prepare" (مهد) a bed is to spread it and make it comfortable. These are the other group who believed and performed righteous deeds (الصالحات). The recompense "they prepare for their own souls" (فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ) is given in the plural form, considering the meaning, just as it was given in the singular in the previous conditional clause "to his own detriment" (فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ), considering the literal wording. In the condition, He sufficed with mentioning righteous deeds without mentioning faith (الإيمان) alongside them, because a deed is only righteous through faith; moreover, faith is mentioned in the following verse.

The meaning is: those who performed righteous deeds—after having faith—prepare for their own souls that by which they shall live and upon which they shall settle.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace. Indeed, He does not like the faithless." (30:45). Al-Raghib said: "Recompense" (الجزاء) is sufficiency and adequacy. Allah the Exalted said: لا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً "no soul shall atone for another soul" (2:48), and He said: لا يَجْزِي والِدٌ عَنْ وَلَدِهِ وَلا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جازٍ عَنْ والِدِهِ شَيْئاً "no father shall atone for his child, nor shall any child atone for his father" (31:33). Recompense is that which provides sufficiency in exchange—if good, then good; if evil, then evil. It is said: "I recompensed him (جزيته) such-and-such." [End of quote].

His saying: لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds out of His grace"—the "lam" (ل) denotes the objective. There is no contradiction between counting what He gives them as a "reward" (جزاء)—which carries the meaning of exchange—and counting it as being from His "grace" (فضل)—which carries the meaning of a lack of entitlement. This is because they themselves, and whatever deeds they produce, are the absolute property of Allah, the Glorified. Thus, they own nothing for themselves such that they would be entitled to a wage. Where is servanthood (العبودية) in relation to ownership and entitlement? Therefore, what they are given as a reward is grace without entitlement.

However, the Glorified, by His grace and mercy, considered them to have ownership of their deeds, even while He owns them and owns their deeds. Thus, He established for them a right which they deserve, and He made what they attain of Paradise and proximity (الزلفى) a wage in exchange for their deeds. This established right is also another grace from Him, the Glorified.

The source of this is His love for them. Because they loved their Lord, they set their faces toward the upright religion and followed the Messenger in what he called toward; so Allah loved them, as He said: قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’" (3:31).

For this reason, the verse counts what Allah gives them of reward as "recompense" (جزاء), which carries the meaning of exchange and substitution, and counts it as being from His "grace" (فضل), considering that this very exchange and substitution is a grace from Him, the Glorified. Its source is His love for them, as hinted at by the conclusion of the verse: إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْكافِرِينَ "Indeed, He does not like the faithless."

From here it becomes clear that His saying: "Indeed, He does not like the faithless" provides the justification (التعليل) for both the negative and positive aspects. That is, He, the Exalted, singles out the believers who do righteous deeds for this grace and deprives the disbelievers of it, because He loves the former and does not love the latter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ آياتِهِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ الرِّياحَ مُبَشِّراتٍ وَلِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَلِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ "And of His signs is that He sends the winds as bearers of good news and to let you taste of His mercy, and that the ships may sail by His command, and that you may seek of His grace, and so that you may give thanks." (30:46). The meaning of the winds being "bearers of good news" (مبشرات) is their announcing the coming of rain when they blow shortly before its descent.

His saying: وَلِيُذِيقَكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ "and to let you taste of His mercy" is conjoined to the position of "bearers of good news" because of the sense of justification it contains. The implied meaning is: He sends the winds to bring you good news and to let you taste of His mercy. "Tasting of mercy" refers to being touched by the various types of blessings resulting from the blowing of winds, such as the pollination of trees, the dispelling of foul odors, the purification of the air, and other things encompassed by the generality of the sentence.

His saying: وَلِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ بِأَمْرِهِ "and that the ships may sail by His command" refers to the movement and blowing of the winds. His saying: وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ "and that you may seek of His grace" means to seek His provision (الرزق), which is from His grace.

His saying: وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ "and so that you may give thanks" is a spiritual objective (غاية معنوية), just as the previously mentioned objectives are formal objectives (غايات صورية). "Gratitude" (الشكر) — the act of using a blessing in a manner that acknowledges the benefactor or praising Him verbally by mentioning His favor — ultimately corresponds to His worship (العبادة). For this reason, it was introduced with "perhaps" (لعل), which denotes hope, as spiritual and conventional objectives may sometimes fail to be realized.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Certainly We sent messengers before you to their people, and they brought them manifest proofs; then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty, and it was a must for Us to help the faithful." (30:47). Al-Raghib said: The root of "crime" (الجرم) is the picking of fruit from the tree... and "he committed a crime" (أجرم) means he became a possessor of crime, similar to "it bore fruit" (أثمر). This was then borrowed for every disliked acquisition, and it is hardly ever used in common speech for a praiseworthy gain. [End of quote].

The verse is like a parenthetical statement, as if it were intended to explain that the believers have a right upon their Lord, which is His helping them in this world and the Hereafter, and part of that is taking vengeance upon the guilty (المجرمين). This right is established by Him, the Exalted, for them upon Himself; thus, it does not entail the problematic notion of Him being overcome in Himself or being subdued and judged by another.

His saying: فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا "then We took vengeance upon those who were guilty"—the "fa" (الفاء) is elided (فصيحة); meaning: some of them believed and others committed crimes, so We took vengeance upon the guilty and it was a right upon Us to help the believers by saving them from the punishment and destroying their opponents. The verse contains some indication that the vengeance upon the guilty is for the sake of the believers, as it is part of the "help" (النصر).

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned," he said: "In the land, it is the corruption of animals when it does not rain—and likewise the destruction of sea creatures because of that."

Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "The life of sea creatures is through rain—so when the rain is withheld, corruption appears in the land and the sea, and that is when sins and acts of disobedience increase."

I say: This is a form of conceptual application (الجري).

In Rawdat al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abi al-Rabi' al-Shami, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ 'Say, "Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of those who were before [you]."' He replied: 'He meant by that: Look into the Qur'an—and observe how was the fate of those who were before you.'"

In al-Majma', regarding His saying: وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِأَنْفُسِهِمْ يَمْهَدُونَ "and those who act righteously, they prepare for their own souls," Mansur ibn Hazim narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: "Indeed, the righteous deed precedes its doer to Paradise—so it prepares for him just as the servant of one of you prepares his bed."

In the same source, a narration came from Umm al-Darda' that she said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) saying: 'There is no man who defends the honor of his brother except that it becomes a right upon Allah to turn away from him the fire of Hell on the Day of Resurrection.' Then he recited: وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ 'and it was a must for Us to help the faithful.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh, from Abu al-Darda'.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحَابًا فَيَبْسُطُهُۥ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ وَيَجْعَلُهُۥ كِسَفًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ ۖ فَإِذَآ أَصَابَ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (٤٨)
It is Allah who sends the winds. Then they generate a cloud, then He spreads it as He wishes in the sky, and forms it into fragments, whereat you see the rain issuing from its midst. Then, when He strikes with it whomever of His servants that He wishes, behold, they rejoice; (48)
وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمُبْلِسِينَ (٤٩)
and indeed they had been despondent earlier, before it was sent down upon them. (49)
فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِ رَحْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٥٠)
So observe the effects of Allah’s mercy: how He revives the earth after its death! Indeed He is the reviver of the dead, and He has power over all things. (50)
وَلَئِنْ أَرْسَلْنَا رِيحًا فَرَأَوْهُ مُصْفَرًّا لَّظَلُّوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ يَكْفُرُونَ (٥١)
And if We send a wind and they see it turn yellow, they will surely become ungrateful after that. (51)
